Bacoo.name: 巴库欧 Bacoo
Bacoo.text:
Bacoo are fey who traveled to the Material Plane to learn more about its inhabitants and became so horrified at what they found that they decided this existence must be a cruel joke. Their method of coping with their epiphany was to join in on the fun, imitating the forms of animals they encountered and becoming mischievous tricksters.

Long ago, bacoo were amphibious fey who dwelled in the twisting branches of mangrove forests. These ancient trees had roots so long that they extended into the Material Plane. Bacoo were awestruck by the natural wonders of their homeland and had inquisitive natures that made them want to explore, but their relative lack of magical prowess made them easy targets. Since they could not travel far from home without being attacked, they instead followed the mangrove roots deeper and deeper until they found a whole new plane of existence.

While they were initially thrilled to discover their magic put them on an even playing field with denizens of this new realm, these bacoo quickly became horrified by other aspects of the Material Plane. The fey realm was a land of plenty, so individuals could do as they liked without fearing the consequences of their own selfishness. Bacoo discovered that death was more impactful and resources were scarcer in the Material Plane, so they expected its inhabitants to be more cooperative and respectful of life. They were wrong. The creatures here were far more vicious and jealous, hoarding whatever scraps they could obtain even when sharing would be more beneficial.

The only conclusion these bacoo explorers could reach was that the gods had played a cruel joke on their creations. They felt the gods had looked at the fey realm and decided to make the Material Plane more miserable in every way. Still, they had made their decision to stay here. Different bacoo adopted varying attitudes toward this revelation. Some sank into despair, others embraced their fey nature to become magical wish granters in exchange for worship, and a few set out to satirize the world in hopes of changing the hearts and minds of its inhabitants.

If you want to play a character with divergent views on the nature of existence, who can be deeply cynical yet constantly see the world in a new light, who is both a carefree trickster and someone who cares about the fates of others, you should play a bacoo.

You Might...

  • Seek new places to explore or manipulate your existing surroundings to experience them anew.
  • Alternate between desiring social interaction and feeling exhausted by it.
  • Challenge the worldview of others to encourage them to consider how they are connected to the creatures around them.

Others Probably...

  • Expect you to be much more transactional and mercenary than you actually are.
  • Assume you are judgmental and that you think yourself superior to them.
  • Think you possess great magical power, such as the ability to grant wishes.

Physical Description

Bacoo were originally shapeshifters in the fey realm but lost part of that ability when they chose to remain in the Material Plane, forcing them to eventually settle into a specific form. As children, they resemble tiny elves with significantly larger eyes and longer limbs. When young bacoo feel strong emotions, most of them subconsciously transform to resemble animals they have seen based on the traits they ascribe to those animals. Unhappy bacoo might take on reptilian forms, feeling cold-blooded and reserved, to help drown their sorrow, while curious bacoo might become amphibious creatures who have the freedom to explore land and water. Their opinions solidify as they mature, and they begin favoring a single form, locking in their appearance when they reach adulthood around the age of 20. Despite their early maturity, their fey ancestry allows bacoo to live up to 800 years or more.

The ability to resemble any single animal they have seen means bacoo appearance varies wildly, though several forms dominate due to cultural influence and constitute the major heritages. Heme bacoo look like bats, kayman bacoo look like caimans, rhizo bacoo look like plants, and scuttle bacoo look like octopuses. All the forms share similarities—they are typically bipedal humanoids with at least two legs and two arms, and their heads remain large and cartoonish with big eyes. They can't change any patterns on their skin, such as stripes or spots, but they can alter their hue within a limited range of colors. Kayman bacoo, for example, can take on any muted grayish shade of color but can't become a vivid or saturated color. Scuttle bacoos, on the other hand, have access to the same range of hues as the octopuses they resemble.

Society

"Practice what you preach" is a core tenet of bacoo society. The criticism they direct at the Material Plane stems from their lived experiences in tight- knit communities. Every member contributes to the greater good by pooling resources and donating their skills. This altruism doesn't come from enforcement but instead arises from recognition that each of them benefited from the same support growing up. Bacoos are actually quite individualistic, and their travels might lead them to different communities over their lives. Newcomers are just as welcome as those who have lived in a place their entire lives, provided they reciprocate the hospitality they receive.

These communities hide from outsiders, revealing themselves only to non-bacoos who earn their trust. Those in the know look for subtle road signs that communities use to direct travelers to them. The signs are usually natural and ephemeral, like placing fallen branches in patterns that look random to passersby or trimming a few leaves in trees so that their shadows cast meaningful shapes on the ground.

For outsiders, they scrupulously maintain an image as mysterious loners. Such deception dates to their history as victims in the fey realm but protects them in the violent Material Plane as well. Bacoos appear in groups when they want to be seen. Places where they visibly congregate, such as trees where kaymans take naps or reefs where scuttles frolick, serve as decoys for the actual location of communities.

Though bacoos stand together in the face of adversity, they don't feel responsible for another bacoo's actions. A bacoo who commits crimes doesn't reflect poorly on their own community, and another bacoo's accomplishments aren't cause for pride except for those who contributed to them. They therefore rarely debate how to govern or force norms on each other. The primary social divisions within bacoo society instead revolve around how they perceive the world. These divisions aren't strict—a bacoo might dabble in multiples simultaneously or change their beliefs over time. Most wouldn't even attempt to categorize themselves under one particular philosophy. Their philosophies are nevertheless helpful in understanding bacoo behavior.

Bacoo Adventurers

The wanderlust-bacoo experience leads many to adventures. They commonly become barbarians, druids, rangers, and sorcerers due to their connection to nature and fey magic, but their inquisitive nature and desire to solve problems also make them good investigators, oracles, and wizards. Bards, monks, rogues, and swashbucklers are also good choices for those who wish to focus on their mischievous nature, stealthiness, and theatricality. Their cultural rejection of higher authority makes champions, clerics, and witches less common, though some bacoo find the power worth the tradeoff.

Explorers

Every aspect of an explorer's life suggests that anything is possible. From their ancestral stories about the infinite potential of the fey realm to their own ability to take a form reminiscent of a creature they've seen, the world is full of wonders that call on explorers to experience them. They also have heightened senses compared to humanoids, allowing them to see more shades of color and making them more sensitive to nuances in taste and smell. This vividness makes them passionate artists and foodies, though many are also prone to having a sweet tooth. Legends about their predilections for bananas, milk, and rum are exaggerated but not that far off the mark.

So while explorers often appreciate the comforts of the familiar—including milk and bananas—they also want to discover what else lies over the horizon. Even the shyest or grumpiest explorer has sneaked away from home one night to exult in the thrill of the unknown, playfully searching over that next hill or around another river bend to see what awaits. Those unable to travel physically let their minds wander instead, becoming voracious scholars.

Isolationists

General sadness about the state of the world pervades bacoo culture. This results in isolationists, bacoo who focus on protecting their own communities. Some are afraid of—or depressed by—the outside world, while others simply find prioritizing their allies to be the most logical pursuit. Bacoos who have spent a lifetime adventuring might also count themselves among this group when they retire to give back to the next generation.

Sometimes even an isolationist community is willing to welcome members of other ancestries who prove to be kind or collaborative. These mortal collaborators might receive an invite from a bacoo friend or "accidentally" stumble into a bacoo settlement when, in truth, the enclave's guards surreptitiously allowed them in.

The size difference between bacoo and other ancestries sometimes leads to bacoo adopting their allies' communities as their own instead. Bacoo who live in the branches of mangrove forests know humans won't find the same environment comfortable but have no problem with human homes. If they learn to trust a nearby village, they might move into the forest around it or even into its attics. Many a "haunted" town is simply home to bacoo who cooperate with neighbors to frighten off unwanted visitors.

Mercenaries

The inquisitive and mischievous nature of bacoo makes them quite suited to mercenary work—the stranger the better. Adventuring mercenaries are almost always up for a quest, provided the reward is appropriate. Mercenaries closer to home typically prefer to cultivate a mysterious and capricious reputation to make their services more desirable. Most of what they do involves simple spellcasting, knowledge of local herbs, or using stealth and subterfuge to perform what is essentially thievery. Tasks include making quick repairs, healing illnesses, and "cursing" people who have wronged others until they make amends. These simple acts appear more impressive than they are because mercenaries shroud them in mystical language and special effects.

Coins or practical goods constitute the bulk of payment for services rendered. Stories about bacoo working for rum, milk, or bananas aren't strictly accurate, but plying a bacoo mercenary with those delicacies can convince them to lower their prices. While some bacoo make mercenary work their entire livelihood, most do it as part of a broader agenda to provide for their community or build relationships with neighboring settlements.

Philosophers

The age-old questions of why the world is so cruel and what can be done about it constitutes an inordinate amount of bacoo conversation, especially for philosophers. Some cultures make small talk about their day or the weather; philosophers prefer bemoaning the stubbornness of gods and their creations.

"What is the amount of wealth that would make the greediest creature satisfied?" They ponder. Or they might ask, "Why do even unintelligent animals kill for sport?"

Sometimes these questions lead to research and spirited debates. Other times, the parties crack a sarcastic joke and move on with their day. Most philosophers internalize any suffering they witness, whether they admit it or not. They might be motivated to promote altruism by example or trickery, or they might succumb to nihilism and immerse themselves in the very brutality they abhor.

Tricksters

Fey tend to have mischievous streaks, and bacoo tricksters are no different. They often enjoy intellectual puzzles and seek out mysteries to solve, but they aren't above base pranks like changing colors and insisting they are someone else. When performing work for others, they tend to find the most complicated way to accomplish a task. They might use divination magic to find a lost trinket, for example, but instead of retrieving it, they orchestrate an absurd series of events that involves hypnotizing wild animals, making crop circles, and entering the dreams of their client to lead the person to the object.

Back when bacoo first reached the Material Plane, their enjoyment of rum led to rumors that they were spirits who lived in bottles. They had more shapeshifting abilities then and found the idea fun. Bacoo tricksters developed magic that allowed them to shrink inside bottles, leaving one finger outside in the shape of a cork plug. Vestiges of this magic remain with bacoo to this day, trickster or not, allowing them to bond with a bottle and hide inside. The usefulness of this trick is debatable, but spies have found it handy to lurk in plain sight. Introverted tricksters consider hopping inside their bottle more socially acceptable than leaving a social engagement entirely, and it is also universally recognized as a great party trick.

Vindicators

Vindicators believe that violence is the universal language of the Material Plane. All the good deeds and kind words in the world mean nothing in the face of might. Thus, vindicators focus their energy on developing their martial prowess.

Most vindicators don't judge other bacoo for disagreeing with them. They instead see themselves as the last line of defense should others fail. Some make a big show of their strength, drawing all the attention and daring foes to challenge them. Some lurk in the shadows and take pride in other bacoo never knowing what they do for the community. They intimidate, blackmail, or eliminate perceived threats before the danger can escalate.

Violence is a double-edged sword, though, and bacoo who make too many enemies go into self-enforced exile. Their subsequent adventures sometimes turn them into folk heroes, but more frequently, they succumb to bitterness and become villains. Stories of vengeful bacoo who attack over minor offenses and enter into agreements whose interpretations they twist like wily devils add to the mystique of bacoo, but within their communities, elders relay them as cautionary tales.

Creatures Of The Night

Bacoos are nocturnal by default but suffer no ill effects from switching their waking hours to daytime. Adventurers and other bacoo who work closely with diurnal creatures adopt their sleep schedule. Otherwise, about a quarter of the population in a given enclave opts to be what they term "day watch," serving as guards or just preferring daylight activities. Day watchers bond with the rest of their community when all are awake but tend to develop their own friendship groups and traditions.

Alignment and Religion

A plurality of bacoo are chaotic neutral, followed by a substantial population that is chaotic or neutral good. Chaotic or neutral evil bacoo exist but constitute a smaller number than the other groups, tending toward the kayman heritage, where they form a plurality. Lawful bacoo are rare.

Due to their disenchantment with the gods, bacoo see religion as a faction they can join and leave at will rather than a higher calling to which they must commit. They follow gods whose goals align with theirs and worship them to obtain powers they need. Some bacoo eschew religion altogether, acknowledging that the gods exist without considering them truly divine. Gods are fallible and some have died, so to those bacoo, gods are simply apex powers in a multiverse of mortal beings. In Alacar, some bacoo prefer faiths that are less reliant on deities, such as the Eld, as opposed to the deities of The Balance.

Bacoo Enclaves

Bacoo settlements are, first and foremost, hidden. Comfort comes second to secrecy, which they consider their best defense. They conceal entrances and protect them with puzzles, traps, and magical wards. The most common settlements consist of tunnels cut into the canopy of trees, the branches of mangrove forests, or underground. That said, bacoo are willing to live with allies they trust. They might lead kobold warrens or move into the attics or basements of humanoid homes. Rumors speak of grand bacoo tree cities shaped from fey seedlings grown in pocket planes of existence. If such enclaves exist, their entrances are such closely guarded secrets that no outsiders have yet reported finding one.

Names

Bacoo do not ascribe significant meaning to names or follow a strict construction for them. They prefer soft- sounding names that remind them of water lapping the coastline, but they also mix in local tones and names that strike their fancy. Bacoo who live among hardriggans, for example, might include multiple hard consonants in their names.

A single name suffices for bacoo. They sometimes add a family name or a geographical surname when introducing themselves if that is the local custom or if they need to distinguish themselves from others with similar names. A family surname is the name of one of their parents prefixed by "sha-" and a geographical surname uses the prefix "lon-" before the name of their home. For example, a bacoo named Lolan with a parent named Benedi who lives in Farlight may introduce herself as Lolan sha-Benedi or Lolan lon-Farlight.

Sample Personal Names

Alam, Dashan, Fosh, Jelani, Lanumo, Lulema, Miliel, Nes, Shosho, Sonal, Wash, Vano.

Bacoo Heritages

Bacoos take different forms based on how they view the world. Each heritage still allows for a wide range of personalities within them and has representation in every aspect of bacoo society.

For example, heme bacoo tend to value community, but individuals have different ideas about how a bat represents them. Some value freedom, imagining themselves soaring through the sky, while others identify with vampire myths and become warriors filled with bloodlust. Choose one of the following bacoo heritages at 1st level.

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.bOUzRx6yOLZozTtc]{Heme Bacco}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.KrC4KFQE2xfqc2U8]{Kayman Bacco}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.gujzmM3U4TAQWgyP]{Rhizo Bacco}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.4RyY6OOc527BQ3Py]{Scuttle Bacco}

Bannog.name: 班诺格 Bannog
Bannog.text:
Bannogs are bipedal, rabbit-like aberrations from the Dimension of Dreams who have a fondness for tricks and games and who navigate life with creativity and resourcefulness. Bannogs cast adrift on the Material Plane live two lives of equal importance: waking life and dreaming life.

Bannogs were originally little more than thoughtforms, figments, and nightmares dreamt into being by sleeping mortals. Yet thoughts know no boundaries or limitations, and so, with time, even dreams can take on a life of their own. Bannogs are one such life form, rabbit-like bipedal aberrations spawned in the Dimension of Dreams—living dreams that possess both a life force and a soul, making them as real and unique as any mortal.

Bannogs resemble rabbits in many ways, as their lagomorphic forms would suggest, including having strong legs for leaping and running, a keen sense of smell and hearing, hyperawareness of everything around them, and a preference for routines. However, bannogs are also dream-, perception-, and fear- shaped beings. These two sides remain in a state of contradiction and flux.

Bannogs are beings both strange and magical, with ears capable of hearing and transmitting psychic vibrations and thoughts, bodies adapted to navigate ever-shifting landscapes and dreamscapes, minds used to parsing the weird and unpredictable, and boundless creativity.

The Dimension of Dreams is a fluid place, composed of the dreamscapes and mindscapes of countless beings around a nexus of semi-permanent sites—wildly differing worlds contained in bubbles of thought that form and burst irregularly. In this oneiric land, the environment of each dreamscape changes alongside a dreamer's emotions, surrounding events are often nonsensical, and life is predictably unpredictable. Time flows irregularly, stopping or racing forward with little outward indication of its passing. Reality within the dream realm is mutable and changes from moment to moment. In the Dimension of Dreams, bannogs can do the impossible with startling regularity. Their ability to alter their environment and themselves is purely mental, and their body's physical limitations are entirely arbitrary. This unique environment has shaped bannogs into adaptable, instinctive people. Bannogs think on their toes, remain unfazed by the strange and confusing events and phenomena around them, and trust their gut significantly more than what their senses are able to perceive.

Amid this dangerous and miraculous world of dreams, bannogs face all sorts of terrifying threats: indestructible nightmares and dreamers gorged on their own freedom and power are just a few of the troubles that bannogs must daily battle or elude. Most dangerous of all are the other denizens, the living beings that frequent the Dimension of Dreams, including night hags, animate dreams, nightgaunts, dramofirs, and the various nightmare beings of Leng. To survive in this harsh environment, bannogs have become hyper-aware of their surroundings and ever vigilant for danger of any kind. Through necessity, they learned how to enter new dreamscapes subtly, scouting and sneaking until they were sure it was safe to explore. Once within a dreamscape, they watch for dream instability, signs of waking, and physical threats. When threatened, most flee, hide, or—if escape is impossible—barter for their lives or offer their services to more powerful beings.

Since bannogs originated as dreams long ago and often occupy the dreams of others, they occasionally slip through the cracks—shunted into the Astral Plane or Material Plane. This can occur upon a dream's ending, through the actions of particularly lucid dreamers or psychics, or when they wander into a mindscape.

Bannogs that reside in the Dimension of Dreams subsist on dream energy, which they absorb from sleeping mortals using a magical membrane that coats their hands and functions like a dangerous spiritual poison. Most bannogs are careful not to drain a single dreamer too much, as this would make the resulting dreams soulless, faded, and dull— thoroughly unappetizing to a discerning bannog. In the most extreme cases, overdraining a dream can even result in the true death of the dreamer, who appears to their mortal friends and family to have simply died peacefully in their sleep. Bannogs are drawn to joyful, exciting, and vibrant dreams, which not only provide the best sustenance but also entertain them and spark their senses of wonder and delight. Bannogs that reside on the Material Plane have adapted to a variety of other foodstuffs and are usually omnivores. This means that they no longer have any need to absorb the dreams of others.

Many bannogs find the Material Plane a rather tedious place to visit, much less to live. Time passes predictably there, and there's no opportunity to speed past the boring or mundane tasks of life. Gravity's a pain, their physical bodies tire, and death is absurdly and unfortunately final. Perhaps most disappointing is that the Material Plane is static. Time, location, and effort are required to change one's surroundings or self rather than being able to change with merely a thought, and many of a bannog's magical abilities are blunted on the Material Plane. To other bannogs, this predictability is a gift—a comforting routine and a sign of safety. Regardless of their home, bannogs remain inexorably creatures of dreams. They fall asleep swiftly and sleep deeply. They recall their dreams with clarity and are lucid dreamers, capable of controlling their dreams and engaging with them actively rather than instinctively. To a bannog, there is no rest in sleep. There is one's waking life and one's dreaming life, and both are equally important.

If you want to play a creative character with a passion for dreams and a yearning for the unpredictable who faces the world with cunning and resilience, you should play a bannog.

You Might...

  • Be creative and enjoy the act of creating tangible and intangible experiences—from designing art, crafting tools, and shaping one's surroundings to devising a clever joke, cutting prank, or challenging puzzle.
  • Take comfort in a daily routine and easily identify patterns in the larger picture.
  • Trust your instincts more than you trust what you see or sense around you.

Others Probably...

  • Believe you're not real—simply a dream or illusion given physical form.
  • Have trouble following your logic but ultimately respect the conclusions you reach.
  • Think you're paranoid, jumpy, or overly cautious.

Physical Description

Bannogs are small aberrations that look similar in appearance to a humanoid rabbit, with sharp nails, pronounced incisors, and human-like ears that stretch well over a foot above their heads. These ears are capable of hearing psychic vibrations and thoughts in addition to sound, and, with practice, a bannog can learn how to use magic to read minds and communicate with others telepathically.

Bannogs have wiry and lean bodies with tightly coiled muscles, quick reflexes, and keen senses. They're fleet of foot and can leap great distances— traits that most bannogs highly value as a reliable survival method and escape mechanism. While on the Material Plane, their fur tends to mimic that of natural rabbits, typically white, gray, brown, black, or the mix of colors typical of a wild rabbit. However, bannogs can appear in any color imaginable during their dreaming lives, changing colors at their whims from dream to dream. Their eyes are most commonly brown, red, or yellow, whether in their dreaming or waking lives.

Mature bannogs of any gender can choose to lay a clutch of absurdly colorful and intricately decorated eggs up to once each year. These eggs are emotionally porous and absorb the emotions from the area around them through their colorful shells, no matter what the emotions might be. For instance, eggs spawned in joyous dreams absorb joy and delight, while those spawned in nightmares absorb fear and malice. While this doesn't dictate the personality of the bannogs born from such eggs, many juvenile bannogs who have fed upon cruelty have a harsh demeanor or pessimistic outlook, while those who've fed upon joy and laughter tend to favor frivolity. This leads some bannog parents to choose specific types of dreams in which to lay their eggs. However, on the Material Plane, the emotional distinction is much more subtle, as emotions come only from the creatures living nearby as opposed to the pervasive, omnipresent emotions of the surrounding dreamscapes.

Within the Dimension of Dreams, bannog eggs can gestate and hatch alone from the riotous outpouring of emotions and thoughts, while on the Material Plane, bannog parents must meticulously tend and care for their eggs, lest they suffer from a lack of emotional sustenance. In exchange for the convenience of hatching there, bannog born within the Dimension of Dreams can't easily leave its boundaries, while bannog born on the Material Plane can experience both their living and dreaming lives equally.

Bannog Adventurers

Driven by strong impulses, bannogs frequently become adventurers. They're natural spellcasters, with most becoming psychics, although bards, oracles, sorcerers, and summoners are not uncommon, the latter of whom dream their eidolons to life. Bannogs who become closely attuned to the world of their waking lives often become druids. Bannogs who lack magical powers are typically rangers, rogues, or swashbucklers. Bannogs rarely take classes that require study or rigid rules. Some popular backgrounds for bannogs include animal whisperer, artisan, artist, bandit, criminal, entertainer, farmhand, hunter, magical misfit, nomad, scavenger, scout, and warrior.

Society

Bannogs are people who have learned through experience to trust their instincts above all else. They're clever and cunning, and they rely on their force of personality and determination to see them through. They place little value on book learning and formal education—they're far too used to the mutable surroundings and inexplicable events of the Dimension of Dreams to care for solid answers and truth. Most are unfazed by the strange or confusing.

Bannogs recognize patterns and routines quickly, and then they either adapt to them or take action to change the routines to suit their own preferences. They carefully craft their own daily and personal routines and take comfort from this small familiarity, even in the midst of larger changes and upheavals. Bannogs are often extremely particular about the methods or order used to attempt particular tasks— perhaps some objects belong in specific places, a minor task must be accomplished in a specific way, or a series of tasks must be performed in a specific order. Despite this strong preference to maintain a daily routine, bannogs are accustomed to being surrounded by the unpredictable and the unknowable. They take surprises and disruptions in stride and dislike stagnation. Routines are comforting yet malleable and can always be changed. A world that never changes is a boring world indeed!

Bannogs enjoy games, tricks, pranks, and puzzles. They're drawn to vibrancy and excitement, yet at the same time, they remain hyper-alert for any sort of danger nearby. They enjoy the act of creation, from tangible arts and trades to intangible artforms, performances, experiences, and—of course—a vividly imagined dream.

Perhaps most integral to bannog society is the role of the concept of dreams. For those in the Dimension of Dreams, dreams are all-encompassing. They're life, self, and reality. But for those on the Material Plane, life is split between the dreaming hours and the waking hours. Both hold equal value. Both are real. To many such bannogs, dreams are liberation—one's body and thoughts are free or unleashed. Yet, the value of waking life is predictability and comforting routine, being grounded in the known, and experiencing the safety of community.

Bannogs are split across the planes and have adapted to life in a variety of ways, forming groups of likeminded individuals. Noteworthy groups found within bannog society include the following.

Bounders

Bounders are travelers, explorers, and nomads— communities that travel in groups across the Dimension of Dreams, the Material Plane, or both realms. The size of these groups varies, from singular family units or clusters of friends to sprawling troupes or large coalitions.

Bounders are highly mobile and make frequent use of scouts, spies, druids, and diviners to ensure the path ahead is safe. They move as a unit, although larger troupes often split into smaller segments as whim, season, danger, or need dictate. Bounders fight to protect their troupe, but as a community, they're always ready to move on or run away when danger rears its head. Bounders are social but cautious, and they range across many environments. Yet, they usually avoid regions of notable danger or concentrations of power, such as large cities, the territory of a powerful monster or tyrant, or a dangerous environment.

Bannog Settlements

Bannog settlements vary greatly by lifestyle. Most bannogs are nomadic, and thus, one's settlement is the troupe itself, along with whatever conveyances or shelters they erect along the way. When bannogs adopt sedentary lifestyles, their settlements are built in a sheltered location with natural features that serve as cover, camouflage, and defensive measures. Bannogs are cautious and always alert for trouble, so carefully camouflaged watchposts and other hunting blinds are common around the settlement's perimeter. Bannog homes are usually communal and built into the ground or landscape in easily concealable forms. Homes are occupied by 2–8 people, usually a combination of partners, friends, clutchmates, and (on the Material Plane) children. Every home contains boltholes, hiding places, and emergency exits to flee not only the home but the entire settlement. These underground features are called warrens.

Dreamspun

Dreamspun are bannogs who strive to gain power over dreams. This power can come in many forms, from staking out territory within the Dimension of Dreams or raiding dreams to creating dreamscapes or becoming a being of physical or occult might. As the boundaries of one's power in a dream are often limited by mental fortitude, imagination, and willpower, most Dreamspun aim to become more cunning, clever, or quick-witted. On the Material Plane, Dreamspun usually yearn to return to the Dimension of Dreams full time and leave their waking life behind, and so they go to great lengths to extend their dreaming hours or traverse planar boundaries. Regardless of their exact desires, Dreamspun aim to become mentally resilient and increase their psychic or occult powers. In order to increase their results beyond what their own efforts can provide, many Dreamspun turn to magic, esoteric beings, foul bargains, or mystical rituals in their efforts. Regardless of how they choose to pursue their goals, most Dreamspun relish crafting, molding, and devouring dreams.

Shadowed

Shadowed live among the fringes, in the shadows of other cultures, groups, or regions. Most stick to small groups or live independently, and they tend to focus on survival. Shadowed are among the most vigilant of bannogs and are always alert for danger and ready to hide.

Many Shadowed are opportunistic scavengers who prey upon others for coin, supplies, or sustenance. In the Dimension of Dreams, Shadowed often live in the spaces between dreams, or the fringes of dreamscapes, where the details are fuzzy, the colors are muted, and predators are fewer. On the Material Plane, Shadowed live on the fringes of urban society, usually in slums, sewers, or in disguise. Sometimes, they serve powerful leaders or join gangs or other social units for safety.

Twinned

While dreamspun bannogs seek power over dreams and wardens delight in their ordinary routine, twinned follow a different path. Most bannogs consider each of their dreaming and waking lives to be important in a different way, but twinned bannogs blur that line, seeking to bring more and more of the waking world into their dreaming life and vice versa. According to twinned philosophy, some sort of deep enlightenment or an even higher state of existence awaits those who can recognize the inner truth of the dichotomy and transcend it.

Thus, to achieve their aim, twinned bring dream logic and phantasmal properties to the waking world, finding ways to morph and tweak the Material Plane to their will. At the same time, they somehow manage to transplant a feeling of routine and normalcy to the Dimension of Dreams.

Most other groups of bannogs find twinned to be eccentric at best, chasing after the greener grass on the other side of the street rather than savoring what they have in the moment. There are even some who feel that the twinned philosophy could be dangerous, somehow upturning the careful equilibrium reached by Material Plane dwelling bannog long ago. The possible negative effects vary from whispered rumor to whispered rumor, but they often center around the idea that twinned meddling might either banish all bannog back to the Dimension of Dreams or else cause the Material Plane and Dimension of Dreams to juxtapose and meld together into a dangerous and unstable mishmash of matter and dreams never meant to coexist.

For their part, twinned tend to be amicable and appreciative of other groups, and their philosophy sometimes makes them more willing to perform the tasks that other bannogs in their community would prefer to avoid. Thus, they get along well with other bannog willing to accept their earnest beliefs for what they are.

Vibechasers

Vibechasers have simple desires, and to fulfill those desires, they seek out sensation, exhilaration, and personal enjoyment. They're determined to create their own fun, instill emotions, and entertain themselves and those around them—for good or for ill. Many Vibechasers are artists, entertainers, performers, or tricksters. Some are bored with their surroundings and seek to make improvements, but others aim to feel a specific emotion or elicit a specific reaction from others. Most Vibechasers are bombastic, sneaky, or both. They tend to enjoy jokes, pranks, or tricks, which vary from lighthearted to cruel based upon the bannog's preferences and outlook. Many Vibechasers utilize illusions or transmutations to enhance their experiences or alter their surroundings. Vibechasers enjoy taking risks, though few care to dwell on the consequences of their actions.

Wardens

Wardens live in tight-knit, sedentary communities and favor safety and routine over excitement and change. Usually, they live in isolation, hiding their community with camouflage and illusory magic. Most Warden communities work together to raise livestock, grow produce, tend fields, and forage. They dig vast underground warrens beneath their homes, which serve as boltholes and hiding places to wait out danger as well as hidden escape routes for emergency use. Wardens are the most likely to stand tall and defend their home, neighbors, or ideals. Yet, when defense becomes untenable, Wardens urge their companions to flee and rally them to start fresh elsewhere. To a Warden, home is important, but the true heart of a community is its people.

Alignment and Religion

Most bannogs are chaotic neutral. They're changeable, adaptable, and driven by whim and desire, with little regard for social mores or local laws. Bannogs span the breadth of morality—from malicious dream raiders and cruel tricksters to light-hearted artists and vigilant defenders. Those who hail from the Dimension of Dreams are somewhat more likely to have malevolent motives, however, while those from the Material Plane bannog are more often neutral or good. Flitting to different opinions and even philosophies like a dream's scattered logic, bannogs are more likely than most other ancestries to change their alignment multiple times during their lives, only settling on a final disposition (if, indeed, they ever do so at all) after numerous phases of reinventing themselves and experiencing how it feels.

Religion, at least as Material Plane folk understand it, rarely holds much importance for bannogs. They instead tend to put more stock in magic and the occult than religious doctrine or divine beings from the outer planes. In the world of Alacar, those who do find faith in The Balance tend to worship Lilin The Mother, god of chaos; Amon the Life of the Party, god of revelry; Sewa the Fool, god of rogues; or Dremira the Visionary, god of the future, for her purview over dreams. Some also worship the Eld, with a special focus on the element of mind.

Bannog Family

Traditionally, clutches of bannog eggs are laid in a dreamscape and left to gestate on their own, meaning that bannogs don't know the identity of their parents, nor do they ever rear their own children. Thus, familial structures are based around clutchmates—bannogs born from the same clutch of eggs. Clutchmates work together for survival and often remain close throughout their lives, with partners and lovers of the various members joining and leaving the clutch as desired. On the Material Plane, egg clutches must be tended, which has vastly changed bannog familial dynamics. There, a group of clutchmates cares for the eggs and juveniles born by all their members, with the clutchmates taking turns laying eggs that will gestate. This increases the number of members of a clutch rapidly and usually necessitates that clutches split over time.

Names

Bannog naming conventions are influenced by countless dreams spawned in the minds of countless cultures and peoples—of course, these names are all warped by the very act of dreaming. Their names are a mishmash of styles and syllables that caught their fancy or were divined from dreams. Lineage and traditional familial ties hold little importance to bannogs, and in fact, few bannog born within the Dimension of Dreams could ever name their parent (if they even met their parent at all), though many could name their clutchmates. For this reason, rather than using family names, they craft titles for themselves that instill fear or inspire joy in those around them.

Sample Names

Banra, Calavash, Elgavag, Fadaine, Gavee, Horgsh, Lomiok, Nivens, Oristar, Petrosh, Simreet, Tuvios, Yulinee, Zerel Sample Titles Delver, Dreamweaver, The Faded, Lightspinner, Realm Walker, Scape Shaper, Terrorborn, The Woven One, Wanderer

Bannog Heritages

Choose one of the following bannog heritages at 1st level.

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.r2FDmnqu7ZjTqb0g]{Bounding Bannog}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.6R7UmmfIpGeyF5gi]{Osmic Bannog}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.EfHVmAgNGvytH80N]{Shadebound Bannog}

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.dMbtTg6QeQJRygEz]{Vigilant Bannog}

Wildfire Leshy.name: 野火莱什伊 Wildfire Leshy
Wildfire Leshy.text:
靛蓝群岛包含种类繁多的生物群系，从干旱沙漠到热带雨林，以及介于其间的一切。这种广阔的生态多样性非常适合创造各种形态的莱西，而几乎每一种传承的莱西都能在岛上的某个地方找到。

各生物群系之间的紧密交互，促使来自每个地区的莱西彼此往来，使得他们总体上比其他土地的莱西更不封闭，并产生了更高比例的莱西冒险者。奇怪的是，尽管这些生物群系中有许多并不会发生野火，野火莱西却是数量最多的传承。但这种丰富并非巧合；在过去的几个世纪里，一个名为“更新之焰之环”的克拉格拉克德鲁伊教团一直在相对大量地创造他们，以应对岛上天气模式变化所造成的失衡。虽然有些野火莱西仍继续直接与该教团合作，另一些则离开教团，踏上属于自己的冒险。关于更新之焰之环及其目标的更多信息，见 @UUID[.u1pBEFgctn6WNrBW]{kragrak}。

野火莱西传承

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.Ut588yPvW1A2oe56]{Wildfire Leshy}

野火莱西是栖居于植被与火焰形态中的自然灵体。他们守望着以火为媒介的重生自然循环，既点燃也扑灭火势，以维持恰当的平衡。野火莱西明白，当森林过久未经历火，最终到来的火灾会燃烧得更久、蔓延得更快、触及得更远，并在其所过之处对植物与动物生命造成远为沉重的打击。在刻意引燃火势之前，野火莱西通常会努力将附带损害降到最低，谨慎选择天气条件，并招募他人的援助，在力所能及之处把当地野生动物引导撤离。

然而，火焰所能做的不止是为植物萌发清理道路。专注于火之破坏性侧面的野火莱西，往往会选择某个对象去对抗并摧毁——无论他们是字面意义上杀死敌人，还是推倒组织与机构。位于亡灵肆虐地区的野火莱西，可能会投身于对抗亡灵；而处在暴政统治之地的野火莱西，则可能致力于煽动叛乱。专注于烈焰之后更新的野火莱西，往往会选择更具复原性的道路，例如建设社区、担任医护，或在艰难的转变时期引导他人。大多数野火莱西将这两种性相都视为同一枚硬币的两面，不过他们各自会在多大程度上体现哪一面，则取决于个人性格与环境。无论其倾向如何，野火莱西几乎普遍厌恶无聊，并在察觉到自己的生活开始停滞时寻求新的挑战。

大多数野火莱西的肉体由德鲁伊在大火之后创造，希望他们能在新生命自灰烬中萌发之际守望大地。另一些则在野火扰动附近游荡的自然灵体、引诱其进入新形态时自发诞生。极少数情况下，不同传承的莱西若被火焰吞噬，会本能地向附近被烧焦的枝叶伸出触及，进而成为野火莱西。这种新生发生得极为迅速，以至于莱西的灵体来不及回到其典型的无形状态并梳理最近一段生命的记忆。相反，新生的野火莱西会保留他们的许多记忆与能力，只在性格或观点上做出少许改变，以更适应其新的形态。

余烬树脂泥怪

自远古以来，野火莱西便与余烬树脂泥怪维系着一种特殊的羁绊；后者是微小而可移动的树脂球体，其核心有阴燃的余烬。鉴于他们与火的共同联系，这种关系往往对双方都有益。一些资料声称，野火莱西创造了最初的余烬树脂泥怪，作为他们从事负责任的森林管理工作的盟友。可以确定的是，野火莱西温暖的身体对余烬树脂泥怪而言尤其是诱人的栖处。许多野火莱西喜欢把这些生物当作宠物饲养，或将一丝魔法的灵智灌注其中，并与它们作为伙伴一同旅行。野火莱西拥有余烬树脂泥怪这一特定魔宠的许可。

莱西族裔是对其灵体而言内在的性格与文化之宽泛分组，并不直接与其特定的物理化身绑定。然而，莱西灵体的族裔确实会影响他们对肉体容器的偏好。在所有莱西族裔之中，最可能选择野火莱西之躯的是游魂者——他们追寻故事与新体验——以及棘火星——他们性急易怒，随时准备投入动作。仪式论者最不可能选择以野火莱西的方式度过生命，因为仪式论者对物体内在力量的专注，以及对小饰物的感伤依恋倾向，可能会与野火莱西作为更新带来者的职责相冲突。

The Indigo Isles contains a wide array of biomes, from arid deserts to tropical rainforests and everything in between. This vast ecological diversity lends itself to the creation of leshys of all forms, and leshys of almost every heritage can be found somewhere on the island.

The close interaction of the biomes encourages leshys from each region to interact, making them less insular on the whole than leshys in other lands and producing a higher rate of leshy adventurers. Curiously, while many of these biomes can't experience wildfires, wildfire leshys are the most abundant heritage. But this abundance is no coincidence; a kragrak druid order known as the Circle of Renewing Flame has been creating them in relatively large numbers over the past few centuries, in order to respond to imbalances created by shifts in the island's weather patterns. While some wildfire leshys continue to work directly with the Circle, others leave the Circle to go on their own adventures. For more on the Circle of Renewing Flame and their goals, see @UUID[.u1pBEFgctn6WNrBW]{kragrak}.

Wildfire Leshy Heritage

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.Ut588yPvW1A2oe56]{Wildfire Leshy}

Wildfire leshys are nature spirits inhabiting a form of vegetation and flame. They watch over the natural cycles of rebirth through fire, both setting and extinguishing blazes to maintain a proper balance. Wildfire leshys know that when forests go too long without fire, the fires that eventually come last longer, spread faster, and reach farther, dealing a far greater blow to plant and animal life in their wake. Before deliberately setting fires, wildfire leshys often look to minimize collateral damage, carefully choosing weather conditions and recruiting aid from others to usher away the local wildlife where they can.

Flame can do more than clear the path for plants to sprout, however. Wildfire leshys who focus on the destructive aspects of fire often choose something to fight against and destroy, whether they do so by literally killing their foes or by tearing down organizations and institutions. A wildfire leshy in an undead-infested region might dedicate themselves to fighting undead, and a wildfire leshy in a land with a tyrannical government might dedicate themselves to fomenting rebellion. Wildfire leshys who focus on renewal after flames, often chose more restorative paths, such as building communities, working as medics, or guiding others through challenging times of transition. Most wildfire leshys embrace both of these aspects as two sides of the same coin, though how much they manifest of each is a matter of individual personality and circumstances. Whatever their tendencies, wildfire leshys almost universally despise boredom, and seek new challenges when they perceive their lives to be growing stagnant.

The physical bodies of most wildfire leshys are created by druids in the aftermath of conflagrations, in the hope that they will watch over the land as new life springs forth from the ashes. Others arise spontaneously, when wildfires agitate nearby wandering nature spirits and coax them into new forms. On rare occasions, a leshy of a different heritage who is consumed by flames instinctively reaches out to nearby burned foliage, becoming a wildfire leshy. This new birth is so rapid that the leshy's spirit has no chance to return to its typical formless state and process the memories of its most recent life. Instead, the new wildfire leshy retains many of their memories and abilities, with a few changes to their personality or outlook to better suit their new form.

Embersap Oozes

Since ancient times, wildfire leshys have maintained a special bond with embersap oozes, tiny mobile balls of sap with smoldering embers at their core. Given their shared connection to fire, this relationship is often mutually beneficial. Some sources claim that wildfire leshys created the first embersap oozes as allies in their work of responsible forest stewardship. What is certainly true is that the warm bodies of wildfire leshys make particularly appealing perches for embersap oozes. Many wildfire leshys enjoy keeping the creatures as pets, or imbuing them with a spark of magical sapience and traveling alongside them as companions. Wildfire leshys have access to the embersap ooze specific familiar.

Leshy ethnicities are broad groupings of personality and culture that are intrinsic to their spirits and are not directly tied to their particular physical incarnations. A leshy spirit's ethnicity does influence their preferences for physical vessels, however. Of all the leshy ethnicities, those who are most likely to take to a wildfire leshy body are wandersouls, who seek out stories and new experiences, and thornsparks, who are hot-tempered and always ready to spring into action. Ritualists are the least likely to choose life as a wildfire leshy, as ritualists' focus on the innate power of objects and tendency toward sentimental attachment to trinkets can conflict with a wildfire leshy's role as a bringer of renewal.

Galtzagorri.name: 加尔察戈里 Galtzagorri
Galtzagorri.text:
靛蓝群岛是一个充满奇观、魔法与谜团之地。由于有许多地方妖精界与群岛之间的帷幕十分薄弱，群岛成为数量惊人的妖精家园，尤其是小妖精（特征）。其中一些小妖精（特征）建立了自己的聚居地，并在物质世界生活了好几代。由于这些因素——以及或许还有一个更强效的秘密或命运在等待整座群岛——靛蓝群岛的小妖精（特征）比其他地方更可能在出生时没有翅膀，注定成为小妖精（特征）中的英雄。

靛蓝群岛的小妖精（特征）为其数量异常之多的冒险者与典范而自豪，而关于这份奇特现象如何发生的“宠物理论”几乎与小妖精（特征）的数量一样多。靛蓝群岛的无翼小妖精（特征）从小就会受到敬重，但这种待遇带来的孤立感往往与鼓舞同样强烈。德拉克西、萤火虫小妖精（特征）、格里格、梅利克西、尼克特拉与皮克西都在靛蓝群岛安家，不过它们的数量仍不及一种更不寻常的小妖精（特征）——永无止境、勤勉不辍的加尔察戈里。

由于靛蓝群岛无翼小妖精（特征）的普遍性，它们在此为“不常见”而非“稀有”，并推荐用于《靛蓝群岛的宝石》冒险。《靛蓝群岛的宝石》的玩家获得小妖精（特征）族裔的许可。

加尔察戈里传承

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.ZpmfVnjRoL2scN2F]{Galtzagorri}

除其他小妖精（特征）传承之外，靛蓝群岛的许多小妖精（特征）都是加尔察戈里。加尔察戈里是细小而焦虑的小妖精（特征），长着瓢虫翅膀；当它们不忙于某种工作时往往会坐立不安，尤其是组装、拆解或修理某样东西。尽管一些学者、甚至一些梅利克西会因为共享的昆虫特征与勤勉而将加尔察戈里归为梅利克西的一个子类，加尔察戈里往往会拒绝这种比较。加尔察戈里指出，它们与工作的关系以及行动模式不同于梅利克西那种先疯狂忙碌、再开派对并陷入糖分崩溃的信条。

尽管总体而言加尔察戈里对钱与现金几乎毫不在意，无翼加尔察戈里却学会了调和一个事实：他人通常会要求用这类无用之物来交换它们继续工作与实现命运所需的有用工具与魔法物品。这使得它们愿意参与此类交换，即便它们认为这是一场闹剧。尽管如此，加尔察戈里冒险者仍乐于至少拥有一位非加尔察戈里盟友来处理财务往来，这样它们就不必为此操心。只要它们需要的有用物品源源不断地到手，它们并不会仔细追究具体是怎么做到的。

加尔察戈里喜欢与任何族裔的同伴组队来完成共同任务，并在追求这类目标时热情高涨，几乎到了过头的地步。这使它们成为冒险队伍中出色的盟友。尽管它们的旅行伙伴有时会被加尔察戈里那种焦虑、鼓劲与强烈投入的古怪混合所扰乱，但有些人最终会觉得这很讨喜。无论如何，加尔察戈里非常乐意承担盟友更想回避的任务与职责，这一点足以弥补因此产生的任何摩擦。

The Indigo Isles are a place of wonder, magic, and mystery. Due to numerous spots where the veil between the realm of the fey and the isles is thin, the isles are home to a surprisingly large number of fey, especially sprites. Some of those sprites have established their own settlements and lived in the material world for generations. Due to these factors, and perhaps a greater secret or fate that awaits the isles as a whole, the sprites of the Indigo Isles are more likely than in other places to be born wingless, destined to be heroes among sprites.

Sprites of the Indigo Isles are proud of their unusual number of adventurers and exemplars, and there are nearly as many pet theories as to how this curiosity occurred as there are sprites. Wingless sprites in the Indigo Isles are treated with deference from a young age, but this can be isolating as often as it is uplifting. Draxies, firefly sprites, grigs, melixies, nyktera, and pixies all make their homes on the Indigo Isles, but they are outnumbered by a more unusual type of sprite, the endlessly-industrious galtzagorri.

Due to the prevalence of wingless sprites in the Indigo Isles, they are uncommon instead of rare, and they are recommended for the Jewel of the Indigo Isles adventure. Players of Jewel of the Indigo Isles gain access to the sprite ancestry.

Galtzagorri Heritage

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-heritages.Item.ZpmfVnjRoL2scN2F]{Galtzagorri}

In addition to the other sprite heritages, many sprites of the Indigo Isles are galtzagorris. Galtzagorris are tiny anxious sprites with ladybug wings who tend to fret when they aren't occupied with some kind of work, particularly assembling, disassembling, or repairing something. While some scholars and even some melixies group galzagorris as a subcategory of melixie, due to the shared insectile features and industriousness, galtzagorris tend to refuse the comparison. Galtzagorris points out that their relationship with work and their patterns of activity differ from the melixie ethos of frenzied activity followed by partying and sugar crashes.

While galtzagorri in general have little regard for money and currency, wingless galtzagorri have learned to reconcile the fact that others typically require such useless things in exchange for useful tools and magic items that they might need to continue their work and achieve their destinies. This makes them willing to participate in such exchanges, even if they consider them a farce. Nevertheless, galtzagorri adventurers delight in having at least one non-galtzagorri ally to handle dealing with finances, so they don't have to worry about it. As long as the useful items they need keep coming their way, they don't look too closely at exactly how it's done.

Galtzagorris love teaming up with others of any ancestry to tackle a shared task and are enthusiastic in the pursuit of such goals, almost to a fault. This makes them exceptional allies in an adventuring party. While their traveling companions might sometimes be unnerved by the galtzagorri's strange mix of fretting, pep, and intensity, some eventually grow to find it charming. Regardless, the way that galtzagorris are more than willing to perform tasks and duties that their allies would prefer to avoid more than makes up for any friction on that front.

Chochori.name: 乔科里 Chochori
Chochori.text:
Chochori are amphibious coral people who have a complex culture with their own unique social groupings, life cycle, and way of looking at the world, distinct from other ancestries.

In chochori society, there are as many myths, legends, and tales of the ancestry's birth as there are storytellers with willing audiences, with few common themes. Were chochori created by the balance's deity of water? Or perhaps they are older than the Balance itself, the children of a powerful and ancient water eldamon. While their earliest history is shrouded in mystery, chochori keep excellent records of what has come after, making their undersea libraries inscribed into shell or other durable materials the envy of other aquatic civilizations. The name chochori comes from the chochori word "cho," meaning all, and "chori." "Chori" is the plural of the singular "chora," indicating a subdivision of chochori with a nuanced meaning that is difficult to translate into a single word.

While each chochori is an individual, a chochori's body is made up of a network of nearly identical polyps, with subtle differences that allow them to perform specialized tasks. Because of this plural nature, as a being composed of multiple interconnected selves working in harmony, chochori are more likely than most ancestries to be plural, a system of individuals sharing the same body. That's not to say that all chochori are plural; it's quite common to see chochori who aren't plural, as well as those of all points on the plurality spectrum, including medians, multiples, and more.

However, chochori don't have a concept of sex or gender, and they have extreme trouble understanding what either of these mean for other ancestries or why they are significant. Especially difficult for chochori are two tasks: they are supposed to tell beings of various genders apart (g'mayuns or orpoks look the same to chochori; other than being able to distinguish individuals, they don't really notice any broader trends) and the use of gendered language, especially in languages that use gender excessively and assign them even to inanimate objects and articles. This is because chochori don't have a gender or sex (some members of other ancestries claim that they do, but that they have a single sex, but most chochori themselves disagree, and thus most people take them at their word). Instead, chochori reproduce in one of two ways: via budding, which creates a child with many similarities to their single parent, or by spawning, where multiple chochori release polyps into the water and some combination of them mix and form infant chochori. Children after a spawning ceremony are raised communally by the chochori community and, unlike chochori born from budding, they are considered to be children of all, rather than any specific parent or parents. In most cases, it would take significant divination magic to even figure out which polyps combined to form the young chochori, and chochori would find such an intrusion offensive, especially if it came with an attempt to claim parentage or some special connection.

If you want to play a character made of coral with an amphibious lifestyle and a distinctive way of looking at the world, you should play a chochori.

You Might...

  • Express a deep curiosity about the stories and mysteries of the world around you.
  • Find it difficult to understand certain things that other ancestries take for granted.
  • Look at everything and everyone around you through the lens of what their chora might be.

Others Probably...

  • Ascribe values or characteristics to you that fit their own conception of what other ancestries are like.
  • Assume that you are focused only on the world beneath the waves.
  • Consider you knowledgeable about the oceans and all of their legends.

Physical Description

Chochori are beings of living coral formed into roughly humanoid shapes. Every polyp making up a chochori's body is a separate but extremely similar component, with different physical features as necessary to serve as part of the chochori's collective body. In this way, they are a single living being made up of a colony of individual components. Those who study chochori biology in a cursory fashion and learn this fact sometimes try to stare at chochori to see if they can find some kind of "seams" or "divisions" between the chochori's different polyps, but they are all connected so seamlessly that it is incredibly difficult to detect with the naked eye.

Chochori adorn and offer to do the same to others for whom they hold affection with all sorts of colorful marine objects (and sometimes even tiny creatures), in a style that makes them look almost like a living coral reef. Chochori tend to add to their adornments as they accomplish deeds, and others who care for them gift them adornments in similar circumstances. This can cause a truly accomplished chochori to have an extremely colorful and eclectic look. Furthermore, some members of other ancestries don't understand where the coral body of a chochori ends and the ornamentation begins, leading them to misunderstand the chochori's true appearance or even causing them to be unable to recognize an extremely familiar chochori with their ornamentation removed or significantly increased. Chochori with a parent, those born from budding rather than spawning, have a deep physical similarity to their parent, but due to the ornamentation, they often have a visual appearance so distinctive that no chochori would confuse them. However, a chochori who intentionally created or pilfered adornments with the explicit purpose of impersonating their parent would have a particularly easy time fooling an onlooker's eyes.

Chochori Adventurers

A chochori's chora has a major influence on whether a chochori is likely to become an adventurer, and what kind of adventurer they might become. For instance, aikrys gravitate towards being alchemists or inventors in order to make use of their ingenuity, while farose are more likely to become champions, barbarians, warrior bards, or other characters who demonstrate courage, passion, or leadership. Chochori of any chora might become clerics, druids, or other religious figures, especially among the Eld where worship is led by a council with one member from each chora. However, even in those classes, hypisks are more common than the other chori. Among chochori, typical backgrounds vary by chora as well. Some popular backgrounds include acolyte, animal whisperer, artist, emissary, entertainer, hunter, scholar, scout, tinker, and warrior.

Society

Chochori society is built around many different dichotomies and tends toward a societal structure they call a choriste, which surface ancestries consider somewhat similar to a "chiefdom," in that it's a mostly collective culture that also includes a small number of hereditary leaders, or a "clan," in that most or all members bear a relationship with each other by lineage. Chochori leaders are the descendants of chochori heroes. Any kind of hero can become a chochori leader, but often those who have hereditary magical or physical gifts, such as sorcerers, have the longest-lived line of leaders. This is because chochori leaders typically reproduce via budding, asexual reproduction with a single parent, passing the mantle of hero-leader on from parent to child. Sometimes old heroic lines die off, and new heroes are born to take their place. Most other chochori reproduce nearly exclusively via spawning, releasing tiny polyps into a common area where they recombine together and form new young chochori. These young chochori born from spawning are raised communally by the chochori settlement or group as a whole, and they don't consider any particular chochori to be more their "parents" than any others. Chochori other than leaders sometimes also reproduce via budding for various reasons, but chochori society considers such a thing unusual and potentially self-absorbed.

Chochori divide themselves into seven chori, subdivisions that most other ancestries find as mystifying as chochori themselves find the alien idea of "gender." Chochori find chori to be perfectly natural and effortlessly subdivide animals, plants, objects, and even members of other ancestries into chori as well. Learning their language (also called Chochori) is incredibly difficult for creatures who can't either instinctively classify things based on chori or carefully memorize predetermined lists of classifications, since adjectives are conjugated based on the chori of the noun they modify, and verbs are conjugated based on the chori of both the subject and the object (for reflexive verbs, the conjugation is the same as when the subject and the object both come from the same chora).

Chori defy easy classification with a single word in Common. A chora is clearly not a social "class" within chochori society, as chochori of every chora can hold any status and occupation. Members of any chora can become a hero and thus a leader. They bear no resemblance to other ancestries' concepts of subdivisions such as ethnicity, and indeed, while a budded chochori child often has the same chora as their sole parent, that isn't necessarily the case. Furthermore, it's possible for a chochori's chora to change during their lifetime, potentially multiple times. This experience is more likely in a chochori who is a gateway system than in a closed system, since gateway systems can have new members enter the system, and the new members might influence the chochori's chora. Chochori don't seem to care that other ancestries can't find a way to classify chori, to them chori are just chori. However, sociologists from other ancestries, desperate to come up with some explanation, have tried to describe them as being more similar to genders than they are to either social classes or ethnicities, in that they are a part of one's identity that is internal rather than being based on heritage or occupation. Even those sociologists realize how much of a stretch this is, and admit that chori are still utterly different from genders and share virtually no similarities, they are the closest concept that they could envision to associate them with. Chochori sociologists, for their part, just see this as another in the endless examples of other ancestries' obsession with gender and sex. The following are the seven chori.

Aikrys

Chochori of the aikrys chora tend to be especially independent, ingenious, and innovative. This makes them likelier than chochori of other chori to be lone pioneers in various fields, especially when it comes to inventing new things or discovering new places. They can be somewhat temperamental and are not always in control of their emotions. The aikrys chora is associated with the element of air, and it is said that a great aikrys hero long ago first discovered that the world of air up above the water's surface was safe for chochori, back in ancient times when it was believed that the surface was the edge of the known world, leading to the underworld or a dangerous otherworld. Aikrys chochori are respected for their ingenuity, but they sometimes innovate and iterate even when the old way of doing things was perfectly serviceable, and sometimes their experiments end in utter failure. That's not to say aikrys chochori act like reckless scientists, with laboratories of exploding prototypes. It's simply the case that it usually takes learning from multiple failed attempts to successfully innovate. Aikrys chochori are among the most common to become adventurers, inventors, and scientists, due to their independent streak and sense of exploration.

Carbor

Chochori of the carbor chora tend to grow in a way that's deeply intertwined with others. Their interconnectedness can make them sympathetic to others' feelings, and their way of considering others' feelings and motivations can also make them talented at persuasion. Carbor chochori can be extremely loyal to those close to them, though they are suspicious and slow to trust when it comes to strangers. However, once they accept someone, they quickly grow closer. The carbor chora is associated with the element of wood, and it is said that a great carbor hero long ago helped set up the structures of the first choristes. Carbor chochori are well-regarded for their ability to understand and get along with others, though other chochori make sure to be open and upfront with carbor chochori in order to prove their honesty and allay the carbor chochori's suspicion. In groups where cohesion and teamwork is crucial, such as hunting parties, diplomatic envoys, or adventuring groups, carbor chochori excel as members, so long as they have time to warm up to the group before a high-stakes mission.

Farose

Chochori of the farose chora tend to be courageous and confident. That's not to say they never feel any sort of doubt or fear, or that they act recklessly. It simply means they are able to continue onward in the face of such fears and doubts with exceptional resolve. They can be enthusiastic in the pursuit of their passions and goals, which is generally a good thing, but can make them impatient when a goal is delayed, especially by delays they feel are unnecessary or pointless. Their brave and decisive attitude can also be seen as aggressive. The farose chora is associated with the element of fire, and it is said that a great farose hero long ago fought back terrifying ancient evils that arose from deep under the sea, ensuring the safety of their homeland. Farose chochori are lauded for their bravery and leadership, but sometimes viewed with anxiety for their tendency towards impatient, aggressive, and occasionally rash solutions to obstacles in their path. No one wants to be seen as an obstacle to a farose chochori. Farose chochori have a tendency to volunteer for fast-paced and dangerous tasks that other chochori would balk at facing. This also includes violent professions such as soldiers or adventurers. They often find roles allowing for leadership or decision-making easier to handle than those where they might have to wait and stew for a decision to be made.

Hypisk

Chochori of the hypisk chora tend to be gentle and artistic, with a calm eye towards the bigger picture and the inner beauty hidden within everyday things. They often have a rich inner world, with deep and expressive dreams. The hypisk chora is associated with the element of water, and it is said that a great hypisk hero long ago had prophetic dreams that led the people of that time to discover rich lands of plenty in which to live and build their settlements. Hypisk chochori are seen as wise for their ability to understand the larger context of things and look past the surface level. However, due to their inner contemplations, they are sometimes seen as reserved or distant, almost as if they're looking past you or through you. Still, they tend to be as sociable as most other chori, so they don't have a reputation for being shy, as vergiss chochori do. Hypisk chochori excel at artistic and creative tasks or those that require deeper reflection and wisdom. They are less likely than chochori of most other chori to become adventurers or other violent occupations.

Jamambri

Chochori of the jamambri chora tend to be quick- thinking and adaptable, able to react extremely quickly and effectively to all sorts of unusual situations. They are often extremely curious, which, when combined with their quick thinking, means that they flit about from curiosity to curiosity, enjoying each one until their inquisitive nature inspires their interest in something else. The jamambri chora is associated with the element of electricity (chochori see electricity as an element, as it is one of the elements in the ancient religion known as the Eld), and it is said that a great jamambri hero long ago was the first to react when chochori of that era met other ancestries, adapting to their strange ways and obsession with incomprehensible concepts like sex and gender. Jamambri chochori are seen as particularly cunning and able to land on their feet in any situation. However, they can also be seen as fickle, mercurial, and flighty due to their ever-changing interests. Jamambri chochori often change their occupation many times throughout their lives, and they try a little bit of everything at least once, except for jobs that require mindless repetition or labor. Jobs with a large number of disparate, quickly-changing tasks or challenges, such as adventuring, are especially popular among jamambri chochori.

Terath

Chochori of the terath chora tend to be patient and equanimous, willing to bide their time and take things slowly if necessary. They are more likely to place extreme value on a promise and oath and to always keep their word. Terath chochori's patience contrasts with both farose chochori's impatience and jamambri chochori's short attention spans. This doesn't lead to any antagonism between terath chochori and those of the other two chori, however. Instead, it allows them to work together well, with each lending their strengths to cover the other's weaknesses. For instance, most terath chochori appreciate the quick thinking of jamambri chochori, while for their part, the majority of jamambri chochori are all too happy to have someone else who is not just willing but eager to perform slow-paced and repetitive work. The terath chora is associated with the element of earth, and it is said that a great terath hero long ago discovered aquaculture, allowing chochori to have a steady, consistent food source through low, careful planning, rather than being subject to the whims of a poor hunting season. Terath chochori are seen as exceptionally reliable due to their tendency to remain calm and patient, and to always keep their promises, even if it requires great persistence to do so. However, their stalwart refusal to back down can sometimes give them a reputation for being stubborn. Once a terath chochori has made a decision, their tendency to stick to it no matter what can be either a virtue or a vice, depending on the situation. Terath chochori often prefer jobs where they can take their time and get everything perfect, rather than those where they have to make split-second decisions or otherwise act extremely quickly.

Vergiss

Chochori of the vergiss chora tend to be analytical and practical, looking at problems from various different perspectives and logically breaking them down into smaller problems in order to solve them. They tend to take things seriously and to keep to themselves, able to relate more easily to logic, mathematics, and other things that follow expected patterns than they are to other people, with their unpredictable moods and whims. The vergiss chora is associated with the element of ice (chochori see ice as an element, as it is one of the elements associated with the ancient religion known as the Eld), and it is said that a great vergiss hero long ago developed the complex mathematics that chochori have used across the ages to research physics and other advanced topics. Vergiss chochori are seen as serious and intellectual and are respected for their logical way of thinking. However, they are often also viewed as shy or introverted. Chochori of other chori might approach a vergiss chochori cautiously and politely when starting a conversation without a predetermined structure for the socialization, out of respect for what they guess to be the vergiss chochori's preferences. Vergiss chochori aren't usually quick to blame others either way. However, while they tend to judge others with exceptional grace and consideration, when it comes to themselves they often hold impossibly high standards, leading them to be extremely self-critical at times. Vergiss chochori's tendency to be introverted doesn't mean that they prefer to work alone, though that's true for some vergiss chochori. Most, however, enjoy working with others nearby as long as the other people are also quietly working and leave them alone with their thoughts so they can concentrate on their own work. They prefer tasks involving intellect, logic, and rationality. This sometimes leads to occupations that are similar to those preferred by aikrys chochori, but while an aikrys scientist is more likely to pioneer a novel research field, a vergiss scientist will usually be the one methodically and logically filling in the conceptual holes left behind during the aikrys chochori's rush to innovate something new.

Chochori Settlements

Chochori settlements tend to be relatively small by the standards of ancestries that build giant metropolises, though chochori see those other ancestries as building settlements that are pointlessly large. Typically, new settlements are formed after the advent of a new chochori hero or heroes, and their lines become the settlement's new leaders. Chochori settlements are places of great learning under the sea, and they are often sought out by other aquatic or amphibious ancestries seeking old tales or new ideas alike.

Alignment and Religion

Chochori tend to have a complicated relationship with alignment that leads to them tending more towards neutral, or alignments at most one step from neutral. This is especially true for chochori who are systems, as the varying perspectives of the differing members of the system make it less likely for the chochori to settle on one of the more extreme alignments. Some of the different chori have mild tendencies towards law or chaos. Aikrys, farose, and jamambri are slightly more likely to be chaotic, and carbor, terath, and vergiss are slightly more likely to be lawful. Hypisk don't tend either way.

While many chochori worship any of the various deities of the balance, much like the other ancestries of the Indigo Isles, a significant number of chochori still worship the ancient elemental religion known as the Eld, which predates the coming of the gods of the Balance to Alacar. Chochori adherents of the Eld consider the seven chori to be manifestations of the Eld and its various different elements, and they tend towards religious structures involving councils of seven high priests, one from each chora.

The Eld

Chochori are hardly the only people on Alacar who worship the ancient pre-balance religion called the Eld, but they're among the more prominent followers in the area around the Indigo Isles, alongside kragraks and some of the various heritages of dragons.

The Eld holds that the world was created from far more inherent elements than the classical few elements on which other elemental traditions focus, with as many as twenty interconnected elements. Rather than anthropomorphic deities, adherents of the Eld venerate the elements themselves, as well as the elemental monsters born of the Eld (sometimes known as eldamon) as their representatives, akin to the role of saints or angels in other religions.

Names

Chochori names tend to be simple and to the point. They almost always contain one letter from their chora in the Chochori alphabet, which is added when they determine their chora during an early coming of age ceremony. It's important to note that some sounds that use two letters in other alphabets, like the "th" in Terath or the "ch" in cho and chora, are a single letter in the Chochori alphabet. When a chochori changes their chora, they also change the letter they added to one from the new chora, unless the letter was present in both chori.

However, chochori generally don't share their personal name except with those they deeply trust, and it's a sign of deep intimacy in chochori culture to do so. Instead, they give acquaintances or new people they've just met an accomplishment name based on their profession or lifestyle, generally based on the most difficult challenge they have ever overcome. For example, a hunter who hunted the seaworm Ripplefang as their mightiest prey might gain the hunter-name Ripplefang, or Seaworm, while a mathematician whose greatest accomplishment was writing a proof that solved the Paradox of Arrows might take the mathematician-name Arrows, Paradox, or Paradox of Arrows. On the other hand, a diplomat whose most impressive achievement was to negotiate the Treaty of Oyster's Rest could have the diplomat- name Treaty of Oyster's Rest.

Sample Personal Names

Avar, Belkri, Erpo, Intha, Lofa, Osmo, Pivo

Sample Accomplishment Names

Banquet of Kings, Concert of the Flowing Tides, Law of Viscosity, Longjaw, Paradox of Arrows, Ripplefang, Treaty of Oyster's Rest

Chochori Heritages

Chochori have distinctive features based on the polyp or polyps from which they are born, completely distinct from the chora they will identify as their own later in life.
