The mist itself isn't magical, but detecting magic as you travel through it reveals bubbles of magical energy floating with no discernible source. These bubbles hum with either abjuration or enchantment magic, though I occasionally sensed faint necromancy as well.

The mist endlessly rises out of Mistvale Rift, a deep chasm slicing across the eastern length of the forest. It functionally separates the rest of the island from the peninsula on which the town of Mistvale sits. Everything is bigger in the Rift, from ancient trees to primeval megafauna that exists nowhere else. Descending into the Rift is like going back in time. The creatures there seem to know they are battling the ravages of time. And they all seem to somehow operate in tandem, as if under the control of a hive mind. Some fly and patrol the forest above for curious travelers who might be tempted to learn more about the Rift than they should know, while others patrol the depths, ensuring nobody returns. Giant tree roots reinforce the rocky walls of the Rift that keep the sea at bay at the ends, and long-tailed enormities swing at rockslides to clear them out, ensuring that the vertical cliffs are difficult for outsiders to descend.

People and Culture

A few groups frequent the Mist, mainly small religious sects that vary from harmless to malevolent, but none live within it. People tend to disappear if they spend too much time in the Mist.

The Children of the Mist are the most well-known sect, though they're relatively harmless. They believe that mist is the fundamental element from which all twenty elements of the Eld derive. Depending on who you ask, their goal is either to unleash the mist across the world or to harness its power for their own use. I've witnessed them attempting to combine various elements through rituals in the Mist, with such attempts always ending in failure so far. Tales of them sacrificing people to the mist or flinging themselves into Mistvale Rift in lemming fashion are, to my knowledge, pure fiction. More believable are rumors that some extremists kidnap eldamon to experiment on them, but the followers with whom I've spoken are horrified at the idea of harming living creatures.

From the outside, the Gray Order appears to be a group of fanatics who conceal their identity with hooded gray robes and worship a demon that they believe to be the source of the mist. I have sources who claim their practices are smoke and mirrors. In reality, they are a guild of thieves who use their phony religion as a cover for their activities. Their calling sign is an iron trinket shaped like a whirling air elemental that emits sulfur-scented smoke. Most people are too distraught at finding the trinket alongside a threatening message to worry about their jewelry that's gone missing. The wealthy hire protection in response to threats that the Order plans to kidnap and sacrifice them—protection in the form of mercenaries in cahoots with the guild.

The most dangerous sect is the one that keeps the lowest profile. The Stilted Court traces its origins to Belledin Muddpool (male orpok alchemist), a former professor at the Academy of Tastes in Seaview who became obsessed with the occult. He led an expedition to Mistvale Rift about thirty years ago to recover what he believed to be a powerful occult artifact that created the mist. Some monster that he claimed to be the Stiltwalker descended upon his team, and he was the only one who returned. He believed that the Stiltwalker would devour all of Bluebell if not stopped, despite possessing no proof it existed. The university humored his claims for a time, sympathetic to the loss of his team, but eventually dismissed him when he neglected his duties in favor of researching obscure magic to combat the creature. Belledin vanished from society a week later, but not before murdering several academy librarians and stealing the restricted tomes they were protecting. The cult believes the ends of protecting the island and its inhabitants from utter destruction justify just about any means. Thus, they'll stop at nothing to accumulate the power needed to conquer Mistvale Rift.

Points of Interest

One road runs through the Mist. Stay on it, and no trouble should befall you. Stray from it, however, and you quickly lose your way. North of Mistvale, the mist is easier to navigate because the canopy is sparser, allowing sunlight to break through the mist on clear days. Follow the sun—or feel which side of a tree trunk is warmest since it's still humid enough for moss to grow all around—to get back to the road.

The south side is a trickier proposition, but lost travelers tend to go in circles and run into the Crying Tree. You hear it before you see it; its branches creak in the wind and sound like a child crying. Despairing travelers also leave notes at its base, creating a memorial to their own demise. Ironically, those who wander away from the Crying Tree are the ones who perish. Rescuers know the Crying Tree is a common landmark and head there first when someone goes missing. The mist also obscures hazards for the unwary, from a snagging tree root that breaks ankles to the sheer drop into Mistvale Rift itself. When lost in the mist, staying put is the best way to stay alive.

Sapphire Meadows.name: 蓝宝石草甸 Sapphire Meadows
Sapphire Meadows.text:
“蓝色的田野是绝美风景，但那靛蓝色泽掩着血色的应得之债。”这是我为农夫鲁尼（男性奥尔波克农夫）写的台词，用在我那出讲述臭名昭著的蓝铃花谋杀案的独角戏里。法庭记录显示他实际说的是：“啊呃，你们这群屠夫！”但那可没这么上口。蓝宝石草甸是蓝铃花的地盘。一望无际的这种多用的花朵，让天空相形之下都显得黯淡。蓝铃花可用来制作染料、工具、衣物，甚至食物。外来者把蓝宝石草甸看作田园天堂：农夫过着安静的乡村生活，又能通过与附近海景镇的贸易得到他们想要的一切。现实更为阴沉。花粉令人沉醉的嗅觉与居民友善的笑容，掩盖了农场之间为种出最好的作物、赢得最丰厚的契约而展开的凶猛竞争。

最初的蓝铃花植株在今天几乎已难以辨认：花更小，茎更细。农夫们花了几十年反复种下那些具有最理想特征的植株所结出的种子，把它们与其他植物杂交，甚至用魔法进行实验。由于蓝铃花会从同一个鳞茎年复一年地再生，蓝铃花制品过去常把自己的血脉当作荣耀来宣传。然而，随着农夫争相栽种更新一代更大、更蓝、更耐性、更美味的品种，这类做法已消失不见。每个栽种季结束时，整片田地都会被连根翻起，鳞茎被剁碎发酵成啤酒或用来点缀沙拉。分级制度会把收成划分为不同品质类别。低等级会以负载方式大量进入市场，而高等级则会被仔细分拣，并放在注满水的桶中运输，以在最新鲜的状态出售。大多数农场会种植各个等级，但也有少数专精于独特品种，以满足特定标准。较小的家族农场为了生存不得不转向这类精品化产品，因为它们无法在价格上与大农场竞争。

人群与文化

汉农农场是蓝铃镇最大的农业企业，也是蓝宝石草甸大多数冲突的挑动者。它在还是汉农矿业一个分支时就开始购买土地。每逢干旱或其他灾害来袭，各农场会集合资源，捐助给受影响最深的人；汉农家却会出面直接买下整座农场，或向那些自尊心太强、不愿接受邻里施舍的人提供贷款。正是这种做法，成为三十八年前那桩蓝铃花谋杀案中指控的根基——那是在汉农矿业崩溃不久之后——五位欠下汉农家巨额债务的知名农夫，在数月间相继死亡。每一案中，他们的家人都不得不卖掉农场来偿还债务。第一位受害者的继承人，也就是如今被称为农夫鲁尼（男性奥尔波克农夫）的人，在其他谋杀发生后对这桩出售提出挑战。他指控汉农家挑动了杀戮，并基于目击者与告密者证词组织出一套极具说服力的案情。案件却因技术性问题被驳回。此后数十年，汉农家不断抹黑他的名声，但他的指控仍在大众文化中流传。

如今，汉农农场拥有蓝宝石草甸近四成的全部农田，并与海景镇政府关系密切，所以别指望他们的扩张会在短期内停下。然而，连年丰沛降雨削弱了他们对领地兼并的推进，于是他们转向两项新策略来让竞争者破产。其一是以亏本价格抛售低等级蓝铃花收成，借此压垮无法承受亏损的小农场。其二是培育蓝铃花新品种，却把种子据为己有，而不是在拍卖会上出售。

这两项策略开始见效，但一位名叫雷勒蒙（女性奥尔波克德鲁伊）的德鲁伊一直用她的魔法复制并传播这些新蓝铃花的鳞茎，然后免费分发给小农场。汉农家把她贴上盗贼的标签，可没人能找到她将其送审，而小农场也完全没有动机去帮助汉农家。图拉克波·花织者（非二元硬里根农夫）则在另一条抗力路径上努力：他带领一群农夫向海景镇请愿，要求在市场实行价格下限，并已在这些努力上取得进展。我怀疑总有一天会出现转折，海景镇政府会醒悟：让一家公司拥有绝大多数蓝铃花作物，恐怕不是个好主意。

兴趣点

蓝铃花传承博物馆是纪念蓝宝石草甸历史的丰碑，最初设在戈尔顿农场的一座旧谷仓里，门票收入的利润会进入救济基金，用于支持小农场。承载博物馆的那座谷仓原本要被拆除，但纳丁娜·戈尔顿（女性奥尔波克农夫）把它与祖父的温馨回忆联系在一起，决定翻修，并做一个关于农场历史的展览。她的邻居们很喜欢这个点子，便捐出了家族往昔的传家物与画作，整个项目也随之壮大。最终，纳丁娜筹资为谷仓不断扩建，直至成为一整座博物馆设施，专门讲述蓝宝石草甸的农耕故事。这座博物馆是岛上备受珍爱的组成部分，以至于汉农农场每年都会捐款给它——尽管博物馆并未以正面笔触描绘他们。

汉农农场经营着一个竞争性的景点，与其说是博物馆不如说更像嘉年华。那里有游戏、表演、爱抚动物园、手工站点等等。这里在家庭游客中极其受欢迎，尽管近期与其提亚拉冬骑乘项目相关的安全事件让它的名声受了打击。园区内还有一座尖端的温室，法师在其中操控内部天气的每个细节，以全年维持完美的生长条件。这使他们能成为某些季节性作物的唯一供应商，尤其是一些热门水果。温室还兼作种子库，收藏了曾被培育出的每一种蓝铃花品种。传言汉农家的施法者创造了新法术，能让他们把这种颗粒度极高的环境控制施加到所有田地上。若属实，这种优势将使他们得以在全岛经济上取得支配地位。

"Fields of blue make a beautiful view, but their indigo hue conceals a bloody due." That's the line I wrote for Farmer Lunny (male orpok farmer) in my one- dragon show about the infamous Bluebell Murders. The courtroom transcript shows his actual words were, "Auugh, ye butchers!" but that's not nearly as catchy. Sapphire Meadows is bluebell country. Field after field of these versatile flowers makes the sky look muted in comparison. Bluebell flowers are used to make dyes, tools, clothing, and even food. Outsiders see Sapphire Meadows as a pastoral paradise where farmers enjoy a quiet, rural lifestyle while getting everything they could possibly want through trade with nearby Seaview. Reality is grimmer. The intoxicating scent of pollen and friendly smiles of residents conceal the ferocious competition between farms to grow the best crops and win the most lucrative contracts.

The original bluebell plant would be barely recognizable today, with smaller flowers and thinner stems. Farmers spent decades replanting seeds from plants with the most desirable traits, crossbreeding them with other plants, and even experimenting on them with magic. Since bluebells regrow year after year from the same bulb, bluebell products used to advertise their lineages as a point of pride. However, such practices have disappeared as farmers race to plant new generations that are bigger, bluer, hardier, and tastier. The end of each planting season sees entire fields uprooted and bulbs chopped up to ferment into beers or garnish salads. A grading system classifies harvests into different quality categories. Low grades go to market in bulk, while high grades get carefully sorted and transported in water- filled tubs to sell at peak freshness. Most farms grow all grades, but a few specialize in unique breeds intended to meet specific criteria. The smaller family farms have had to transition to such boutique products to survive, unable to compete with the bigger farms on price.

People and Culture

Hannon's Farms is Bluebell's biggest agricultural enterprise and the instigator of most conflicts in Sapphire Meadows. It began buying land when it was just a branch of Hannon Mining. Whenever drought or other disaster struck, farms would pool their resources together to donate to those most affected. Hannon's would instead step in to buy the farm entirely or offer loans to those too proud to accept charity from their neighbors. The latter practice underpinned the accusations in the Bluebell Murders case that occurred thirty-eight years ago—shortly after Hannon Mining collapsed—when five prominent farmers who owed substantial debts to Hannon's died within months of each other. In each case, their family had to sell their farm to pay off the debts. The first victim's heir, known today as Farmer Lunny (male orpok farmer), challenged the sale after the other murders occurred. He accused Hannon's of instigating the killings and assembled a compelling case based on eyewitness and whistleblower testimony. The case was thrown out on a technicality. Hannon's has spent the subsequent decades smearing his reputation, but his accusations endure in popular culture.

Hannon's now owns nearly forty percent of all farmland in Sapphire Meadows and has a cozy relationship with the government of Seaview, so don't expect their expansion efforts to stop anytime soon. However, years of bountiful rainfall have put a damper on their territorial acquisitions, so they've turned to two new strategies to bankrupt competitors. The first is selling low-grade bluebell harvests at a loss to price out smaller farms that can't absorb the losses. The second is developing new breeds of bluebells but keeping the seeds to themselves rather than selling them at auction.

The two strategies are starting to yield results, but a druid named Rellemun (female orpok druid) has been using her magic to replicate and disseminate bulbs of these new bluebell plants and then hand them out for free to smaller farms. Hannon's has branded her a thief, but no one can locate her to bring her to trial, and none of the smaller farms have any incentive to help Hannon's. Tulahkpor Blossomweaver (nonbinary hardriggan farmer) is pursuing another avenue of resistance by leading a group of farmers petitioning Seaview to implement price floors in their marketplaces and has started to make headway with these efforts. I suspect that something will give one day, and the government of Seaview will wake up and realize that allowing one company to own the majority of the bluebell crop probably isn't a great idea.

Points of Interest

The Bluebell Heritage Museum is a monument to Sapphire Meadows' history that started in an old barn on Gordun Farm, and profits from ticket sales go to a relief fund supporting small farms. The barn that houses it was supposed to be demolished, but Nadinna Gordun (female orpok farmer) associated it with fond memories of her grandfather and decided to renovate it and make an exhibit about the farm's history. Her neighbors loved the idea and donated heirlooms and paintings from their families' pasts, and the whole thing grew. Eventually, Nadinna raised funds to add to the barn until it became an entire museum facility dedicated to the story of farming in Sapphire Meadows. The museum is such a beloved part of the island that Hannon's Farms donates to it every year, despite the museum not painting them in a favorable light.

Hannon's Farms runs a competing attraction that's less museum and more carnival. It has games, shows, a petting zoo, craft stations, and more. The place is extremely popular with families, though recent safety incidents with its thyaladon rides have put a dent in its reputation. There is also a cutting-edge growhouse on site where wizards control every aspect of the weather inside to maintain perfect growing conditions all year. This enables them to be the sole supplier of some seasonal crops, especially some popular fruits. The growhouse doubles as a seed vault, hosting every variety of bluebell that has ever been developed. There are rumors that Hannon's spellcasters have created new spells that would enable them to apply such granular environmental controls to all their fields. If true, such an advantage could allow them to achieve dominance over the whole island's economy.

Violet Woodlands.name: 紫罗兰林地 Violet Woodlands
Violet Woodlands.text:
紫罗兰林地的美会让你屏息凝神——无论是比喻意义上还是字面意义上。浸润万物的浓郁紫色来自树叶中的一种毒素。该地区以树叶为食的昆虫随着时间推移适应了将毒素融入体内，而它们的捕食者也随之适应。如今，紫眼狐狸追逐着紫耳野兔穿过长着紫水晶荆刺的灌木丛。淡紫熊撕裂鱼肉时，鱼血如酒般倾泻。山猫的皮毛上有着苋紫色的条纹，在它们的狩猎中提供伪装。只要你不吃任何紫色的东西，基本就能避开这种毒素。生活在林地里的少数有毒生物，主要是蜘蛛与蛇，往往会逃离类人生物而不是狩猎它们。不过秋季很危险，因为树木会在雨季前散落紫色花粉。吸入花粉会使毒素在体内累积，很快导致疾病甚至死亡。少量会引发类似食物中毒的症状，但暴露超过几个小时就可能导致肺部迅速衰竭并死亡。

然而，只要某样东西存在，你就可以确信一定有人试过吃它。恰如其名的绞杀藤是一种寄生植物，会从其攀附生长的树木上窃取养分，并以稀释的量从宿主处累积毒素。蓝铃岛上少数主厨已精通一项技术：以恰到好处的量从绞杀藤中提取汁液，使毒素不至于致死。把它作为酱汁加入菜肴时，会在舌头上产生刺痛感，并导致轻微幻觉。我的巨龙生理足够强韧，以至于我尝试那道菜时没察觉任何不同，但与我同席的用餐伙伴报告说他们看见火花在我们的表上迸裂，并声称我的声音“尝起来像苹果”，这表明轻度毒素诱发了联觉。

生活在紫罗兰林地的毒素eldamon也具备独特属性，与世界其他地区相同元素的eldamon有所不同。然而，与其结交是不可能的，因为它们已经在与kragrak训练师合作，而这些训练师专注于单一职责：阻止紫罗兰林地扩张。kragrak口述根源描述了一个紫色曾是蓝铃岛主导颜色的时代。根据这些传奇，迷雾曾是对紫罗兰毒素不具抗性的生物的庇护所，但当毒素也开始渗入迷雾时，居民发起反击，焚烧大片森林，尽管他们明知烟雾会杀死许多人。传奇进一步宣称，当时的德鲁伊尊重自然的所有形态，并不想根除林地。他们与蓝铃岛的eldamon交流，并承诺与之合作维持岛上的平衡。于是便诞生了奉献的奠基神话：一个与紫罗兰林地的毒素eldamon缔结羁绊、并操控紫罗兰毒素以阻止其蔓延的eldamon训练师组织。

人群与文化

许多xotlxotl来到紫罗兰林地，以毒素来考验自身的体质。大多数人打算只停留片刻，而那些鲁莽到不认清自身极限的人会死去。然而有一群人，在大约二十年前征服了毒素。他们在赫拉蒙湖定居，湖水使他们免受花粉侵扰，并通过谨慎且反复的暴露，把毒素整合进自己的生理之中。他们的后代拥有明亮的紫罗兰色皮肤，以及深邃皇家紫的鳃茎。赫拉蒙xotlxotl比其同族更为放松，或许是毒素致幻特性的副作用。大多数xotlxotl彼此相处时给人的感觉像是在霸凌，而赫拉蒙xotlxotl更像是竞技对手。根据你的出身，这两者可能差别不大，但我坚持认为确有区别。有些人还具备天生的占卜能力，被称为预言者。每年花粉季都会举行一场年度仪式：外来的xotlxotl穿越紫罗兰林地，试图跃入湖中；而赫拉蒙xotlxotl则试图用大棍把他们击退。预言者会赠予那些成功入湖的外来者关于其未来荣光的解读。

具有妖精传承的生物似乎也对毒素具备一定韧性，包括bacoo与小妖精（特征）。他们在林地东南部的一套洞穴系统内建立了一个小村落，名为紫湾。居民能容忍外来者，但大多数人搬到那里，是因为他们不愿与类人生物互动。他们过着以社群为导向的生活方式，人人分享所有物，且在很大程度上自给自足。极少数需要与外界交易的场合，他们会制作无与伦比的精美染料——包括紫色系之外的颜色。此类贸易远征每三到四年才会发生一次，发生时会成为盛大的庆典。Bacoo会带着数辆装满染料的货车前往Hinter，在镇子边缘开店，直到存货售罄。

春季的一场年度狩猎会吸引来自蓝铃岛各地乃至更远之处的访客。森林里栖居着一种名为Grovekeeper的神话生物。据说它有麋鹿的身体、山羊的头，以及巨龙的翅膀——当然，全都是紫色。Grovekeeper会在鹿群数量激增的年份出现，带领它们为觅食而对附近社群发起横冲直撞。狩猎会控制鹿的数量，防止它们饥饿或攻击，否则可能在林地内外造成严重生态破坏。若这些措施失效，狩猎也会将目标指向Grovekeeper，使其停止协调鹿群。猎人曾多次杀死Grovekeeper，但它总会归来。有人认为它是一只无法真正死亡的古老eldamon，但我认为新的Grovekeeper会通过某种原始或秘法过程，从普通鹿中诞生。

兴趣点

冠脊路是穿越紫罗兰林地的唯一路径。它在Hinter与Crest之间南北延伸。道路沿线每四分之一英里就立着一个约小谷仓大小的检查站。这些建筑对花粉密封，并储备面具、淡水与解毒剂小瓶等物资，以确保旅人能活着走完旅程。Crest与Hinter共同资助维护道路的建筑工会。奉献会阻挠修建额外道路的尝试，称需要尽可能保留紫罗兰林地的自然状态。在xotlxotl成功定居后，他们为通往赫拉蒙湖的一条未铺设小径破例放行。他们警告说，进一步试图驯化紫罗兰林地，可能会打破林地与蓝铃岛其他区域之间脆弱的平衡，导致森林再次开始扩张。

不过，冠脊路沿线也有若干值得注意的地点，而沿路施工并不会引发奉献的愤怒。除了bacoo之外，还有谁能发明一道有毒菜肴并说服人们去吃呢？Luroosh（雌性bacoo主厨）是一名scuttle bacoo，毕业于味觉学院，同时取得烹饪学与化学双学位。她拥有胃痛旅舍，这是一家高档酒馆与旅店，坐落在紫罗兰林地北界内四分之一英里处。她在此发明了让紫罗兰毒素可食用的工艺，并在数百名试图学习该技术的厨师中，仅认证了另外四人，使他们能在蓝铃岛各地的餐馆提供这种料理。旅舍是探索紫罗兰林地的绝佳基地，因为其彻底密封以防花粉侵入，客人可以安全地用餐与睡眠。

奉献在道路穿过林地的中点设有一处访客中心，在那里向旅人讲解此处独特的生态系统，并讨论为何尊重其生态平衡至关重要。中心也会主持年度狩猎，以将Grovekeeper控制在可控范围内。希望离开冠脊路徒步的人，往往可以在中心找到kragrak向导协助。奉献通常会按行程距离收取费用，但也可能为协助怀有高尚目标的冒险者而免除费用。

这些目标通常涉及关闭可能在紫罗兰林地开启、并通往妖精界的传送门。没人确定这些传送门为何会出现，但它们可能导致不友善的妖精生物入侵，寻找可征服的新领地。若奉献无法自行处理局势，他们会呼吁援助，而高尚的冒险者与寻求挑战者往往会响应号召。若任由恶意妖精在紫罗兰林地立足，一旦它们积攒足够力量形成据点，并开始打击附近聚居地，结果只会是灾害。在冠脊路南端与胃痛旅舍相对的位置，有一座供奉平衡三位神祇——Jata、Sewa与Sumaworo——的神殿，该神系被称为“三则故事”。这座神殿俗称传奇，会协助旅人，并作为仲裁的中立地点。传言称相当一部分神职人员是灰色教团分支的成员，但显然没人会承认。

The beauty of the Violet Woodlands will take your breath away, both figuratively and literally. The rich purple that suffuses everything comes from a poison in the tree leaves. Insects in the region that ate the leaves adapted over time to incorporate the poison into their bodies, and their predators adapted in turn. Now violet-eyed foxes chase purple-eared hares through bushes with amethyst thorns. Fish blood pours like wine as lilac bears tear through their flesh. Amaranthine streaks run through the fur of bobcats, camouflaging them on their hunts. You're mostly safe from the poison if you don't eat anything purple. The few venomous creatures that live in the woods, mainly spiders and snakes, tend to flee from humanoids rather than hunt them. Autumn is a dangerous time, though, as trees shed their purple pollen prior to the rainy season. Breathing in the pollen causes toxins to build up in your body, which can quickly lead to sickness or even death. Small amounts lead to illness with symptoms similar to food poisoning, but exposure for more than a few hours can lead to rapid lung failure and death.

However, if something exists, you can be assured someone has tried to eat it. The aptly named strangling vine, a parasitic plant that steals nutrients from the tree on which it grows, accumulates poison from its host in dilute amounts. A select few chefs on Bluebell have perfected the technique of extracting sap from the vine in just the correct quantity for the poison not to kill. When added to dishes as a sauce, it creates a tingling sensation on the tongue and leads to mild hallucinations. My dragon physiology was hardy enough that I didn't notice anything different when I tried such a dish, but my dining companions reported seeing sparks erupt across our table and claimed my voice "tastes like apples," suggesting that the mild poison induced synesthesia.

The poison eldamon who live in the Violet Woodlands also possess unique attributes distinct from those of the same element in other parts of the world. However, befriending one is impossible because they are already working with kragrak trainers who are focused on a single duty: preventing the Violet Woodlands from expanding. Kragrak oral traditions describe a time when purple was the dominant color of Bluebell. According to these legends, the Mist was a sanctuary for creatures that weren't resistant to violet poison, but when the poison began infiltrating the Mist as well, its residents struck back, burning swaths of the forest despite knowing the fumes would kill many. The legends go on to claim that the druids of the time respected nature in all its forms and didn't want to eradicate the woodlands. They communed with the eldamon of Bluebell and promised to work with them to maintain balance on the island. Thus proclaims the founding myth of the Dedication, an order of eldamon trainers who bond with the poison eldamon of the Violet Woodlands and manipulate the violet poison to prevent its spread.

People and Culture

Many xotlxotl come to the Violet Woodlands to test their constitution against the poison. Most intend to stay for a small while, and the foolhardy who don't recognize their limits die. One group, however, conquered the poison about twenty years ago. They established residence in Lake Hullamon, where the water insulated them from the pollen, and through careful repeated exposure, they integrated the poison into their physiology. Their descendants have bright violet skin and gill stalks that are a deep royal purple. Hullamon xotlxotl are more relaxed than others of their ancestry, perhaps a side effect of the hallucinogenic properties of the poison. Where most xotlxotl come across as bullies to each other, Hullamon xotlxotl act more like sporting rivals. There might not be much difference between the two, depending on where you're from, but I insist a distinction exists. Some also possess innate divination abilities and are called augurs. There's an annual ritual during pollen season in which outsider xotlxotl run through the Violet Woodlands and try to dive into the lake while Hullamon xotlxotl attempt to beat them away with large sticks. The augurs gift outsiders who make it into the lake with readings about their future glories.

Creatures with fey heritages also seem to have a degree of resilience to the poison, including bacoo and sprites. They have a small village based out of a cave system called Purple Cove in the southeastern portion of the woods. The residents tolerate outsiders, but most move there because they don't wish to interact with humanoids. They have a community-oriented lifestyle where everyone shares what they have and is largely self- sufficient. On rare occasions when they have to trade with the outside world, they manufacture beautiful dyes of unsurpassed quality—including colors other than shades of purple. Such trading expeditions happen only once every three or four years and are occasions for great fanfare when they occur. Bacoos bring several wagons of dyes to Hinter and set up shop at the edge of town until they run out of stock.

An annual hunt in the spring brings visitors from all over Bluebell and beyond. The forest is home to a mythical creature called Grovekeeper. It's said to have an elk's body, a goat's head, and a dragon's wings—all purple, of course. Grovekeeper appears in years when the deer population soars and leads them on rampages through nearby communities in search of food. The hunt keeps the deer numbers in check so they don't starve or attack, which could otherwise cause severe ecological damage in and around the woodlands. Should such measures fail, the hunt also targets Grovekeeper so that it stops coordinating the deer. Hunters have slain Grovekeeper on numerous occasions, but it always returns. Some believe it is an ancient eldamon who can't truly die, but I think new Grovekeepers arise from regular deer through some primal or occult process.

Points of Interest

Crest Road is the only path through the Violet Woodlands. It runs north and south between Hinter and Crest. Checkpoints about the size of a small barn stand every quarter mile along the road. These buildings are sealed against pollen and contain supplies such as masks, fresh water, and antidote vials to ensure travelers survive the journey. Crest and Hinter jointly fund the construction unions that maintain the path. The Dedication hinders attempts to build additional paths, citing the need to preserve as much of the Violet Woodlands in its natural state as possible. They made an exception for an unpaved trail to Lake Hullamon after xotlxotl successfully settled it. They warn that further attempts to tame the Violet Woodlands could tip the tenuous balance between the woodlands and the rest of Bluebell, leading the forest to begin expanding again.

Several notable sites exist along Crest Road, however, and construction along the road doesn't raise the Dedication's ire. Who else but a bacoo could invent a poisonous dish and convince people to eat it? Luroosh (female bacoo chef) is a scuttle bacoo who graduated from the Academy of Tastes with dual degrees in gastronomy and chemistry. She owns Stomachache Lodge, a high-class tavern and inn that sits a quarter mile inside the northern boundary of the Violet Woodlands. Here she invented the process to make violet poison edible and certified four other chefs—out of hundreds who tried to learn the technique—to serve it in their restaurants around Bluebell. The lodge is an excellent base for exploring the Violet Woodlands, as it is thoroughly sealed to prevent pollen intrusion, allowing guests to dine and sleep safely.

The Dedication maintains a Visitor Center at the midpoint of the road's path through the woods, where they educate travelers on the unique ecosystem and discuss why it's important to respect its ecological balance. The center also hosts the annual hunt to keep Grovekeeper under control. Visitors wishing to trek off the Crest Road can often find a kragrak guide from the Center to assist them. The Dedication typically charges a fee based on distance traveled but may waive it to assist adventurers with noble goals.

Such goals typically involve closing portals that can open in the Violet Woodlands that lead to the fey realm. No one is sure why such portals occur, but they can result in incursions by unfriendly fey creatures looking for new territory to conquer. The Dedication calls for assistance if they're unable to handle the situation themselves, and noble adventurers and those looking for a challenge often answer the call. Allowing malevolent fey to gain a foothold in the Violet Woodlands can only result in disaster if they can build up enough strength to form a foothold and begin to strike nearby settlements. Mirroring the placement of Stomachache Lodge on the southern end of Crest Road is a temple dedicated to the Balance's trio of deities, Jata, Sewa, and Sumaworo, the pantheon known as the Three Stories. The temple, known colloquially as The Legends, assists travelers and serves as a neutral site for arbitration. Rumor has it that a significant portion of the clergy are members of the Gray Order sect, but obviously, none will admit it.

The Wild Bluffs.name: 狂野峭壁 The Wild Bluffs
The Wild Bluffs.text:
崎岖的狂野峭崖拥有风铃草岛上最多变、最不宜居的地形。斜坡随处可见，由嶙峋的突岩构成，使每一步都充满风险。跌落会在锋利的熔岩岩石边缘上留下割伤与擦伤——而那还是你走运的情况。若翻滚得足够远，你会先摔下一个悬崖，再摔下另一个，经历一连串几乎垂直的坠落，如同沿着痛苦金字塔一路下降。然而，这些峭崖却是有翼者的天堂。每到冬季，候鸟便成群结队地聚集于此。巢穴遍布崖壁与翻涌海浪间的孤立石柱。许多鸟类都十分罕见，在人口稀少的北方极地与靛蓝群岛这处偏僻角落之间往返迁徙。迁徙每年都会吸引观鸟者来到风铃草岛，而当局也必须提防野生动物走私者，他们试图偷走鸟蛋或雏鸟，卖给私人动物园。

持续的火山活动塑造着这些峭崖。尽管火山已有一段时间未曾喷发，熔岩仍会从水下喷口涌出，地震也会重塑峭壁。有些熔岩流在过去的喷发中持续更久，并在最终停止时留下地下隧道。这些熔岩管道纵横交错穿过峭崖，只要没有在近期地震中坍塌，它们就可能比地表路线更安全。

尽管危险重重，仍有许多农夫、矿工与其他匠作之人居住或在狂野峭崖工作。这里的人口比篮子地区或蓝宝石草甸更为稀疏，但仍相当可观。这里的环境尤其适合葡萄种植，群岛中一些最负盛名的酒庄便坐落于此。

人民与文化

若没有攀爬者库拉赫科尔（男性哈德里甘探险家）绘制的详尽熔岩管道地图，狂野峭崖的生活将艰难得多。库拉赫科尔对攀岩与洞穴探勘的热情促成了风铃草登山协会的成立——这是一个为志同道合的运动者设立的休闲俱乐部。他们远征所需补给的成本很快就会累积起来，因此他们会为探索过的区域测绘，并出售地图来资助自己的爱好。有些熔岩管道宽敞到足以让马车穿行，使得一些若从地表前往便无法抵达的地点也能建立聚居地。

登山协会的许多成员也属于鸟类学家协会，不过这种关联或许是因为观鸟爱好者若不学会攀爬狂野峭崖，就无法从事他们的嗜好。该协会在克雷斯特南方设有一座小屋，自称是群岛最伟大的自然史博物馆，甚至超越了伦普兰克的皇家藏品。他们拥有来自所有岛屿的动植物标本，连月影岛以及环绕三大主岛的诸多小岛也不例外。计划探索月影岛奥秘的冒险者，最好先到小屋做个预览，看看前方会遇到什么。

狂野峭崖的居民多半独立自持，倾向于独来独往，或只与同样行当或兴趣的人往来。穿越这片区域的艰难意味着，只有习惯独处的人才会往这个方向冒险。不过，有一个共同兴趣将各个群体联系在一起：对火山的由衷敬畏。克雷斯特的修道院会追踪流经火山的魔法能量，并每月发布更新。

至少每年一次——若他们认为情势需要则会更频繁——修道院会在山顶召集一次采集会，以进行降温仪式来防止再次喷发。该魔法仪式的材料成本极为可观，且需要一周时间才能施放。居民借此机会聚在一起，捐出自己的时间与钱，参与这或许是世界上最庄严的节庆。施法者轮流交替，以便有人能在他人提供后勤支援并为他们鼓劲时得以休息。人们也认为在仪式期间举办体育竞赛能带来好运。根源认为，向艾尔德与平衡之神祇展现此等生命力，能鼓励他们守护火山周遭的生命。

兴趣点

一棵孤独的蓝花楹树矗立在一处半岛的尖端，制图师认为那里是风铃草主岛的最南端。当地人将此处昵称为日端，因为你可以坐在树荫下，看太阳既从海面升起，也沉入海中。传奇称，杰西亚·迅箭在前往建立雾谷的途中曾侦察过此地，并认定这里不适合定居，却是藏匿宝藏的绝佳地点。树干上刻着的一个“X”，其外观与杰西亚著名伤疤相似，为这段传奇增添了佐证，尽管至今无人在附近发现任何宝藏。据说，随着太阳横越天空而移动的树影便是线索。考虑到杰西亚作为传奇弓手的名声，我推测树影指引人将箭射入某个触发，但至今仍无人解开谜题——若它确实存在的话。

强大的魔法地脉线流经狂野峭崖，很可能正是火山过去剧烈活动的原因之一。登山协会公开提供的地图标示了最靠近地表的安全路线，但能证明自己实力的冒险者则可获得更深层熔岩管道的地图——那些通道一路下行，直抵地脉线本身。库拉赫科尔将这些隧道称为辉光熔炉，因为其中黑曜石岩壁会以虹彩般的魔法能量发出辉光。工匠能在那等深处驾驭魔法，为物品灌注独特性质——前提是他们能在危险中存活。黑曜石大地元素在辉光熔炉中游荡，举止如同蜂巢里的工蜂。这些大地元素会收集有机物——不论死活——并将其置入一条地脉线中，用岩石将其包覆；随后地脉线会逐渐吸收这些物质，并将其转化为魔法能量以强化元素。攻击其中一只元素，或让它们察觉到你的存在，都会引来其他元素前来援助，因此潜行是生存的关键。专精隐形法术的人常会带工匠前往辉光熔炉，但即便有这类防护，也并非所有人都能回来。

The rugged Wild Bluffs have the most dynamic and inhospitable terrain on Bluebell. Slopes abound and consist of jagged outcrops that make each footstep a risk. Falls result in cuts and scrapes against the sharp edges of lava rock, and that’s if you’re lucky. Tumble far enough, and you go off one cliff, then another, in a sequence of sheer drops like descending a pyramid of pain. The bluffs are a paradise for those with wings, however. Migratory birds flock here every winter. Nests cover cliff faces and isolated pillars among the churning waves. Many are rare sights, splitting time between the sparsely populated Arctic north and this remote corner of the Indigo Isles. The migration attracts bird watchers to Bluebell every year, and authorities have to watch out for wildlife traffickers looking to steal eggs or hatchlings to sell to private zoos.

Ongoing volcanic activity shapes the bluffs. While the volcano hasn’t erupted in some time, lava does flow up from underwater vents, and earthquakes reshape the cliffs. Some lava flows persisted longer during past eruptions and left underground tunnels when they finally ended. These lava tubes crisscross the bluffs and can be safer than surface routes, provided they haven’t collapsed in a recent earthquake.

Despite these dangers, many farmers, miners, and other tradespeople live or work in the Wild Bluffs. The population is sparser than that of the Basket or Sapphire Meadows but still substantial. The environment is particularly suitable for grapes, and some of the islands’ most renowned wineries are here.

People and Culture

Life in the Wild Bluffs would be much more difficult without the detailed maps of all the lava tubes in the area created by Kurahkkor the Climber (male hardriggan explorer). Kurahkkor’s enthusiasm for rock climbing and spelunking led to the founding of the Bluebell Mountaineering Association, a recreational club for like-minded athletes. The cost of supplies for their expeditions adds up quickly, so they chart the areas they explore and sell the maps to fund their hobby. Some of the lava tubes are large enough for wagons to drive through and enable settlement of sites that would be unreachable via the surface.

Many members of the Mountaineering Association also belong to the Ornithologist Society, though perhaps the correlation exists because birding enthusiasts can’t engage in their hobby without learning to climb the Wild Bluffs. The Society has a lodge south of Crest that claims to be the greatest museum of natural history in the Isles, surpassing the royal collection in Rumplank. They have specimens of flora and fauna from all the islands, even Moonshadow and the many smaller islands surrounding the main three. Adventurers planning to explore Moonshadow’s mysteries would do well to stop by the lodge to preview what’s in store.

Residents of the Wild Bluffs tend to be the independent type who keep to themselves or only engage with others who share a trade or interest. The difficulty of traversing the area means only those comfortable being by themselves would venture this direction. One common interest unites all the groups, however: a healthy respect for the volcano. The monastery in Crest tracks the magical energies moving through the volcano and issues monthly updates.

At least once a year—but more often if they feel the situation warrants it—the monastery calls a gathering at the summit to conduct a cooling ritual to prevent another eruption. The magical ritual has a substantial material cost and requires a week to cast. Residents use the occasion to come together and donate their time and money to what is perhaps the world’s most solemn festival. Spellcasters switch in and out to take turns resting while others provide logistical support and cheer them on. It’s also considered good luck to hold sports competitions alongside the ritual. Tradition holds that such demonstrations of vitality to the Eld and the gods of the Balance encourage them to preserve life around the volcano.

Points of Interest

A lone jacaranda tree stands at the tip of a peninsula that cartographers believe to be the southernmost point of Bluebell’s main island. Locals nickname this spot Sunpoint because you can sit under the shade of the tree and watch the sun both rise from and set into the ocean. Legends claim that Jesea Swiftarrow scouted the area on her way to founding Mistvale and decided that while it wasn’t suitable for settlement, it was an excellent place to hide treasure. An "X" carved into the trunk of the tree that matches the appearance of Jesea’s famous scar bolsters the legend, though no one has yet found any treasure in the area. The tree’s shadow as the sun moves across the sky is supposed to be a clue. Given Jesea’s reputation as a legendary archer, I presume the shadow points the way for one to fire an arrow into a trigger, but none have solved the puzzle yet—if one exists at all.

Strong magical ley lines flow through the Wild Bluffs, most likely contributing to the volcano’s past violent activity. Publicly available maps from the Mountaineering Association show the safest routes closest to the surface, but adventurers who prove their mettle can obtain maps of deeper lava tubes that descend into the ley lines themselves. Kurahkkor calls those tunnels the Glowforge because their obsidian rock walls glow with iridescent magical energy. Crafters can harness the magic in those depths to imbue items with unique properties, provided they can survive the dangers. Obsidian earth elementals wander the Glowforge and behave like drones in a hive. These earth elementals collect organic matter—dead or alive—and place it in a ley line, encasing it in rock while the ley line gradually absorbs the matter and converts it into magical energy to empower the elementals. Attacking one of the elementals or alerting them to your presence causes others to come to its aid, so stealth is crucial to survival. Those specializing in invisibility spells will often run crafters to the Glowforge, but not all come back, even with such protections.

Prominent Bluebell Settlements.name: 著名的蓝铃花聚居地 Prominent Bluebell Settlements
Prominent Bluebell Settlements.text:
关于蓝铃岛上各个聚居地，我还有更多要说的，但我觉得佩尔博里议员的《欢迎来到海景》宣传册实在迷人，所以我们不如先一起看看那个吧？

I have more to say about the various settlements here on Bluebell Isle, but I find Councilor Pelborii's weclome brochure to Seaview simply charming, so why don't we look at that together first?

Seaview.name: 海景 Seaview
Seaview.text:
这里是佩尔博里·卡尼哈尔！我是一名31岁的格马尤恩女性，兴趣包括信息、秘法艺术，以及世界事件背后更深层的原因——那些有权有势的人有时会试图阻止我们发现真相。哦，还有在海滩上长途散步，但前提是要沿着一条地脉线，而且群星得排列得当。我是海景城议会的一员，代表非奥尔波克的利益，所以关于这座城市我有很多话要说。别忘了在即将到来的议会选举中投我一票！让我带你看看我的城市！

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-journals.JournalEntry.FjubW3kX88aRvA0D.JournalEntryPage.HXm5gKmFxX1C0yvr]{佩尔博里的海景城欢迎宣传册}

Pelborii Karnihar here! I'm a 31-year-old g'mayun woman with interests in information, the mystic arts, and the deeper causes behind world events that powerful folk sometimes try to stop us from finding out. Oh, and long walks on the beach, but only as long as it's on a ley line and the stars are aligned. I'm a member of the Seaview city council, representing non-orpok interests, so I have a lot to say about the city. And don't forget to vote for me in the upcoming council election! Let me show you my city!

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-journals.JournalEntry.FjubW3kX88aRvA0D.JournalEntryPage.HXm5gKmFxX1C0yvr]{Pelborii's Welcome Brochure to Seaview}

Crest: The Mountain Sanctum.name: 徽记：山岳圣所 Crest: The Mountain Sanctum
Crest: The Mountain Sanctum.text:
Crest

Settlement 5

  • N
  • Town

Small monastic mountain town built on a volcano.

Government Town Council

Population 824 (29% kragraks, 15% hardriggans, 13% orpok, 9% g’mayun, 8% leshies (especially wildfire leshies), 7% sprites (especially galtzagorris), 6% bacoo, 5% aeternal dragons, 4% bannogs, 4% others)

Languages Auran, Common, Draconic, Ignan, Jotun, Orpok, Sylvan, Terran

Religions Xthe Balance, the EldX

Threats fire monsters, rockslides, volcanic activity

Earth and Fire Nexus Crest is located at the convergence point for earth and fire ley lines, which increase the quality of related items available. Characters can locate items with the earth or fire trait, or that cast spells with the earth or fire trait, of up to 12th level for sale in Crest’s shops or the monastery.

Artokra female kragrak druid and priest of the Eld

Cinders at Dawn agender wildfire leshy elemental avatar

Karshekk male hardriggan union head

Lasha male heme bacoo subterranean eldamon arena impresario

Hurry up and gather together. I have places to be and trinkets to sell. Let's get this over with as quickly as possible. You can call me L'orle, master merchant and preeminent peddler. My trade takes me to every nook and cranny of Goldcrop, so aren't you the lucky one to learn what took me decades in just a few hours? Let's get started.

See that single volcano standing at the eastern edge of Bluebell, towering over the Sapphire Meadows? That is why farming is so prevalent here—the exceptionally fertile volcanic soil from that very volcano. While the volcano isn't so tall as to jut past the treeline, it still stands out amid the relatively flat lands to the west and north and even looms over the Wild Bluffs to the south. Near the mountain's peak lies quite an unusual site: the town of Crest, named for the location and centered around a large but austere monastery dedicated to the Eld, that ancient religion that worships the elements. It might come as a surprise to hear it, but the settlement of Crest sprang up around the monastery slowly over time. Despite some reticence from the monastery staff, they slowly became pillars of the new community as the local traditions shifted and blended together.

But not everything has changed since the time of the solitary monastery. One tradition remains sacred to this day to every inhabitant of Crest, whether or not they're adherents of the Eld: the volcano itself bears no name. According to ancient records kept by the ancient kragrak priests who used to tend the monastery, calling upon the mountain's name or giving it a new one and speaking it awakens the spirit within the mountain, presaging an eruption. Whether true or not, the townsfolk are worried enough about the potential disaster to follow the old stories and repeat them across the island.

Legends say the first kragrak monks built the monastery many centuries ago after a violent eruption that spread further than ever across the island and wreaked much havoc, yet it gave Bluebell Island its legendary fertility for crops. The monks sought not only to meditate over the mysteries of earth and fire but also to understand how the earth and fire ley lines intersected at the monastery's location to predict or even prevent future eruptions.

The monks lived alone in peace for some time, never bothering the other island inhabitants, but after they successfully prevented another eruption, they gained tremendous popularity as stoic, taciturn, but resolute protectors. Despite the quiet and meditative monks' complete lack of interest, a tiny settlement sprung up around the monastery of admirers, miners, merchants, magical crafters, and researchers hoping to use the ley line powers. When this village had conflicts, these conflicts troubled the monastery, and so the monks agreed to mediate disputes when requested, if only to preserve their own peace and quiet. It might have seemed like a good idea at the time, but if their goal was to stay aloof, the result was the complete opposite. Over time, the advisory role of the monks was codified into a town council led by the monks' appointed representative. The monks didn't have a formal leadership structure to speak of, as they were more interested in self-guided and peer-assisted introspection, and the representative was seen as less of a prestigious position of power and more as a tedious obligation by most among their number… and that brings us to today, when that tiny village has grown into a small town populated by hundreds. What will the future bring for Crest? Even the mountain is uncertain, and they say that it has birthed a daughter who will be a harbinger of great bounty, or great destruction.

Life in Crest

Today, people in Crest tend to be split into three groups, each with a distinctive style of conduct, style of dress, and daily routine.

First are the monks and their many fans, as well as other faithful of the Eld, who are easy to pick out from the crowd. They wear simple clothing, rise with the dawn, spend the night in quiet contemplation, and generally tend to be unassuming and non-confrontational. While kragraks are a plurality of both Crest's population in general and the Eld faithful specifically, they are overrepresented among this group, though still not by enough to put them in the majority.

Second are the miners and lumberjacks, here for the rare ores, minerals, and magic-rich volcanic rocks, as well as the rare trees that have grown on the mountain's highest slopes. While the more quiet and respectful nature of Crest as a whole isn't entirely lost on these brave and brawny gatherers, they still tend to be rougher and rowdier than any of the other groups in town, and most incidents of public disorder arise among them. They also engage the most ardently in the town's nightlife and are tolerated for their incredible stories and the colorful "entertainment" they bring to the town. Many of Crest's sizable hardriggan population are members of this group.

Finally, there are the wealthy members of Crest, generally magical crafters and researchers. This group also includes several merchants who buy up valuable raw materials and magic items and ship them elsewhere, bringing back needed items via overland routes as well as landing ships among the fishing villages on the nearby coast east of the mountain. This is the smallest group, and unlike in many other places where the rich wield their wealth as a carrot or a stick to maintain true power, in Crest, they hold markedly less influence than the monks of the Crest Monastery.

Add to this mix a constant stream of visitors, most common among them farmers from the rich and fertile fields at the mountain's base, who climb to sell their goods at Crest's famous market days in exchange for interesting treasures the merchants brought in from all around the Isles.

The push and pull of Crest is a story of the clash between tradition and quiet contemplation on the one hand and innovation and raucous growth on the other. Even now, it's clear the settlement isn't sure what it wants to be. For instance, many inhabitants believe the elements are sacred, to the point that the town formally refused to fund the construction of an arena for Eldamon battles like many other towns, seeing it as "unseemly." Of course, that didn't stop an enterprising bacoo from opening up his own subterranean arena in a nearby cave, an open secret among the townsfolk that has grown to become extremely popular in recent years. Nowhere is this dichotomy more physically apparent than in the experience of the wildfire leshy Cinders at Dawn, who came across a unique eldamon he called Mountain's Daughter, the embodiment of a branching future not only for Crest but for Bluebell Island as a whole.

People of Crest

Crest is a mix of various ancestries, though the kragraks, the original inhabitants of Crest Monastery, are the most populous. The three groups of inhabitants, the faithful, industrial workers, and merchants and mages, don't separate entirely into cliques but intermingle to a greater degree than you might expect. You might find an extended family with members from all three who still gather together weekly for dinner and conversation.

Crest Council

The governing body of Crest, the Crest Council, is at once extraordinarily influential while possessing little official power. It's quite fascinating, really. Most political structures involve some sort of enforcement arm of soldiers or guards employed by those in power. In Crest, the monks who serve as the nucleus of the settlement are trained in self-defense but choose not to exercise that strength aggressively. Instead, the council makes rulings and decisions that are officially only suggestions, and a volunteer constabulary marshals up against those who violate the peace, at least so long as enough people are enthusiastic enough about the decision to volunteer their aid. I have to tell you, the first time I heard about this system, I was worried it meant the town was ruled by mob justice, but the monks serve as a check on such activity, unwilling to tolerate unprincipled action that's taken nominally in their own name. When a true threat to the settlement arises, the monks join the volunteers.

The council's number of members varies over time. When Crest has a citizen who the populace believes is respected enough to join the council, someone nominates them and gathers signatures on their behalf until they have enough to join. Interestingly, the council has no size limit, so the number of members is always in flux. While there aren't necessarily term limits, council members resign when they feel ready to do so, and the council expels members for blatant ethics violations. The way things work, the council is a microcosm of Crest as a whole.

The most influential council member, who might as well be its leader despite her own lack of ambition, is Artokra, a kragrak druid and priest of the Eld. While ash kragraks have a reputation for being impulsive, I found Artokra to be stoic and dignified. She's still as mentally and physically flexible as any ash kragrak, but she's possessed of such great wisdom that she always makes those traits an asset rather than a hindrance. It's likely not an exaggeration that her steady hand has allowed Crest to continue with its informal system during recent growth and change.

Cinders at Dawn, an agender wildfire leshy, is the newest member of the council and, much to their own dismay, has a faction trying to maneuver them into toppling Artokra for informal leadership. The reason for this is Mountain's Daughter, an exceptional eldamon who merged with Cinders' spirit, granting Cinders incredible fire powers. The way Cinders tells the story, they were wary of a volcanic eruption and traveled to the caldera, where they witnessed Mountain's Daughter emerging. Tired from her volcanic birth, she found Cinders a nice place to rest. Cinders has managed to manifest a sliver of Mountain's Daughter's power as a familiar and uses that to commune with her. In so doing, they have gained great influence, and many in Crest believe that Cinders holds the key to their future.

Worker's Union

The miners and lumberjacks of Crest belong to an unusual but highly effective organization called the Worker's Union, which came about due to the monastery's ownership of the nearby land, including several with an incredible wealth of minerals and rare trees. The monastery refuses to sell the land to wealthy investors, and they also allow its use, generally free of charge, to those who agree to respect the land. This arrangement allows independent miners and loggers to work as they please without having to toil away for a wealthy mine owner and keep their profits based on what they can deliver to the market.

However, over time, the workers realized that this arrangement didn't grant them any safety net and forced them to negotiate with merchants on their own. To combat this, they formed the Worker's Union, which collects a small amount of dues but, in exchange for membership, provides a support network for injured workers and negotiates favorable deals for members to purchase needed equipment and sell the fruits of their labor. Karshekk, a late-blooming hardriggan man and a shrewd negotiator, serves as the union's head. He makes sure no one takes advantage of his fellow members, and he is an ardent advocate for folks in the union to find their own paths and truths, even if that's different from what their family expects.

Exploring Crest

The following is a sample of some of the prominent locations found in Crest, both within the town limits and in the nearby mountain area.

Crest Monastery

While Crest Monastery is located on the western edge of town, not in the middle, it serves as both the social and spiritual center that holds the town together. The monastery would certainly be smack dab in the middle of things if that's what they had wanted, but they originally asked for the first settlers to build their homes some distance away, and it's been creeping toward the monastery ever since. Today, the two are essentially connected. Here, the adherents of the Eld follow that religion's philosophy of self-sufficiency and self-enlightenment, with a bent toward the elements of earth and fire, which are mystically prevalent in the local region. The monastery houses a beautiful meditation garden with several sections, one of which is entirely made of stones and sand. I've heard those sorts of gardens are a kragrak tradition.

Lasha's Arena

Eld worshipers are so independent that you can never tell their opinion on the matter until you ask them, but it turns out that the monks don't really appreciate eldamon arena battles, despite their growing popularity in larger settlements. They feel like it's too much spectacle, and even though eldamon enjoy fighting and don't get hurt during these battles, these arenas commercialize the sacred. They appreciate personal eldamon battles; they just don't like the idea of a big arena with paid spectators.

Respecting their wishes, the town never built an eldamon arena like the ones you'll see in some other cities, but that didn't stop the enterprising heme bacoo Lasha from making an underground arena—literally. The existence and location of Lasha's Arena are an open secret, so it's easy enough for even a newcomer to find the cave entrance, where the bat-shaped bacoo has set up a surprisingly comfortable establishment in a large subterranean cavern with more amenities than you might expect. His husband, Sithniu, a nyktera sprite— they might both look like bat fey, but don't get them confused; nykteras are a lot smaller than heme bacoo—is in charge of providing food and drink and is an excellent host. Some patrons even come for the meal more than the entertainment, and it's likely the couple could run a successful inn instead if that's what they wanted.

The Peakmarket

If you're here to buy or sell, chances are good that you're headed to the Peakmarket. It's a testament to Crest's popularity, as well as its relatively central location among the nearby coast, farmland, and other regions, that Crest's high-altitude Peakmarket has managed to remain the key trading hub of eastern Bluebell rather than ceding that honor to one of the smaller villages directly on the coast. Rare ore and wood are for sale here, and if you want to buy earth or fire magic, chances are the Peakmarket is your best bet! You can also sometimes find great prices on produce from the farms surrounding the unnamed mountain, but Crest's own inhabitants often purchase food from the same farmers in regular shipments with set amounts and prices, so if that's what you're looking for, the best time to come is when they have a surplus harvest.

Union Hall

The Worker's Union, especially under Karshekk, has a motto to prioritize its members above its own operating costs, at least as long as they can still perform their expected roles. As such, Karshekk attempts to go under budget whenever possible and keep a little coin for a rainy day while returning the extra to the members. You can see this in action if you visit their headquarters, as the front facade and the rooms where the members meet with merchants look attractive and polished. But if you head into the back, you'll see them cleverly reusing an old door as a table, wooden buckets as stools, and other cost-saving measures.

Zenith Watch

While Crest is named for how close it is to the peak of the mountain, the true peak and its caldera are still above. There's a small observatory called Zenith Watch located there, an unusual building that serves the purposes of the various citizens of Crest. Mages and researchers studying the volcanic energy use one portion of Zenith Watch as a laboratory, while monks who have come to attune with the summit's elemental power have a separate section where they can meditate in peace.

Hinter: Breadbasket of Bluebell.name: 腹地：蓝铃的粮仓 Hinter: Breadbasket of Bluebell
Hinter: Breadbasket of Bluebell.text:
Hinter

Settlement 6

  • N
  • Town

Market town with countless festivals.

Government Elder Council

Population 4,192 (41% orpoks, 39% g'mayun, 12% bacoo, 8% others)

Languages Auran, Common, Orpok, Sylvan

Religions the Balance, the Eld

Threats raucous revelers, thieves

Market Festivals Due to Hinter's many festivals designed to increase trade, there's almost always more to buy there than in other towns of the same size and stature, but the offerings are haphazard depending on the festivals' timing. A character who spends a day shopping in Hinter's markets and looking for a common item of up to 11th level can attempt a DC 11 flat check; on a success, they find the item. On a failure, it's not available in Hinter right now, though at the GM's discretion, they can try again in a month or so.

K'lark female g'mayun elder and possible prophet

Chamberlain Olg nonbinary scuttle bacoo master of ceremonies contest referee, judge, and constable

Grand Garley male orpok eating contest grand champion

As an itinerant peddler, I've hawked my goods in every community across the face of Bluebell. There is no better place to sell my wares than Hinter, and no pleasanter place to while away an evening afterward. While on the road, I count the nights until I'm in Hinter once more. Despite its name, Hinter is no unimportant backwater. Much of the food that weighs down Seaview's dining tables arrives by way of Hinter. If Seaview is Bluebell's stomach, Hinter is its mouth.

Every morning, well before dawn, carts groaning with produce make their way toward Hinter from every nearby farming town and village. Farmers herd droves of thyalodons and other livestock into pens on the town's outskirts; fishing boats from Mistvale drop off their daily catch; and local hunters and adventurers bring in strange and exotic game. They then supplement all these foodstuffs with the bountiful fruits of Hinter's local farms and the offerings of traveling tradespeople (including my humble self!) until, finally, the great wagon train starts for Seaview—a holy pilgrimage setting off towards a cathedral to culinary connoisseurship. The enormous convoy—nicknamed the Chow Train—begins their journey early, even before the dawn rises, traveling all day and part of the next in order to reach the great city of Seaview in time to make its way to tomorrow's breakfast.

However, Hinter wasn't always the farm to Seaview's table. When orpok first arrived on the island, Hinter was a self-sufficient g'mayun farming settlement, much larger than the Seaview of old. But as Seaview flourished, so did its logistical requirements and Hinter's trade opportunities. The reluctance of Crest's monks to grow the settlement and its relative lack of farmland compared to Hinter meant that even though the distance was greater, Hinter was poised to dominate the supply.

In the old days, each of the island's many farming villages had its own trading relationship with Seaview. But somewhere along the way, some ambitious Hinter elders struck upon a clever way to cut themselves a bigger piece of the pie, both figuratively and literally— and the famous Hinter market was born. Each day, Hinter's town square becomes a bustling fairground, a market where a farmer can find a ready buyer for their produce and then shop for what's needed back home: a new scythe from Nilbog, a Hinter-baked bluebell pie, a comb, and some pretty ribbons manufactured in Seaview. The vast majority of the market is filled with foodstuffs, but if you look around hard enough, you can probably find the non-food item you need at the Hinter market.

After shopping, a visitor can find plenty of reasons to linger in Hinter—to watch the pie-eating and wrestling contests (these are usually, but not always, two separate events), partake in a harvest festival, or tip back a brew at the Fiddler's Green.

Life in Hinter

The first time I visited Hinter was during a festival. In fact, every time I've visited Hinter, it's been during a festival. With seasonal fairs, religious holidays, market days, and a different harvest festival celebrating each crop, there aren't many unmarked days in Hinter's calendar. Only Rumplank exceeds Hinter when it comes to the quantity of parties, and there are bitter rivalries as to which of the two boasts the higher quality of those parties.

While every day in Hinter is special, here are some of the highlights—the best of the best. I believe all of them are worth a special trip to see at least once in your life.

Village Fair Days: Hinter sets out special days dedicated to each of the small farming communities on the island. Residents of the chosen village are feted, fed for free, given discounts at the market stalls, and loaded down with trinkets for the little ones. On a village fair day, nearly the entire population of the chosen settlement passes through Hinter's gates, enjoying a true holiday—and leaving behind a good deal of their extra spending money.

The No-Fair Fair: On no-fair fair days, Hinterlanders set up empty booths for visitors to sell their goods. For a change, Hinter locals (and shoppers from all over the island) buy the products of Bluebell's other communities. Even Seaview comes out in force, with artisans, chefs, and craftspeople displaying their own wares. I arrange my travel to make sure I never miss a no-fair fair day, and I always manage to end the day with a peddler's two greatest blessings—an empty pack and a full purse.

Melonfest: Bluebell's sweetest produce, the noble ryemelon, is a complementary staple to many of its cold foods, the main ingredient in ryemelon ale, and a delicious dessert in its own right. This humble melon's whole week of riotous celebration is a family favorite. Ryemelons grow big in Bluebell, and their massive size is the basis of many contests: the melon roll, the melon smash, melon carving, the melon growing contest, and, of course, the melon chuck, where crowds watch the island's strongest hurl the massive projectiles from Melonchuck Hill.

The Bluebell Games: If there's a busier week than Melonfest, it must be the Bluebell Games. This is the island's greatest sporting competition. Great crowds gather to watch the wrestling, archery, and illusion competitions, with a purse of gold for the winners of each event. The biggest draw is the eating competition, where the most voracious eaters test their mettle against Grand Garley.

While Hinter's celebrations are varied, they all share some signature sights, sounds, and smells. Sun-faded bunting adorns stalls, and the colorful splashes of first-, second-, and third-place ribbons mark contest winners ranging from wrestling champions to prize pumpkin growers. Hawkers' shouts mix with the crowd's cheery cacophony, and Chamberlain Olg's announcements echo through the fairgrounds. And over it all float the mouth-watering aromas of fried payayo and breaded catfish.

There's lots of fun to be had in Hinter, and outsiders sometimes imagine its residents leading lives of carefree merrymaking. Anyone who moves there pursuing a life of leisure is doomed to be disappointed. It's hard work throwing a party every day (on top of a full day of farming, carting, or marketing!) Most Hinterlanders are up before dawn, doing their chores, and getting ready to host the stream of travelers from all over the island. Farmers, marketers, and entertainers work steadily through the day.

Only as night falls and the stream of visitors slows to a trickle do the locals close their booths and start their own merrymaking. Each night is a party as the native Hinterlanders (and honorary citizens, such as myself) celebrate another successful market day. Best of all, in Hinter, unsold fair food isn't wasted! Instead, marketers carry it to the tables beneath the town's Feeding Tree. The groaning planks, used during the day to host the town's famous eating contests, are put to much the same use at night. Once the sun has set, there's plenty of gourmet fare to whet the appetites of even the most demanding orpok palate, and the rum flows freely from the taps at the Fiddler's Green and the other taverns throughout the town.

While everyone loves a party, no one in Hinter celebrates in quite the same way as the town's bacoo population. Bacoo-led singalongs and jamborees often last far into the night! Hinter's sunset celebration, smaller and more intimate than the ones during the day, is just as joyous.

People of Hinter

Hinter's permanent population (primarily orpok, bacoo, and g'mayuns) makes up perhaps a fifth of the crowds that throng its markets.

Among the town's notable inhabitants are its elder council. Every resident with a working tenure of more than ten years has a voice on this council (although few bother to attend the meetings).

The most respected inhabitants carry the most weight in the council, and none is more respected than K'lark (female g'mayun elder). A venerable g'mayun with snow-white feathers, K'lark is whispered to possess a powerful gift of magical prophecy, but in fact, her oracular wisdom may be nothing more than an uncommon portion of common sense and a wealth of experience. When K'lark invites listeners to "mark my words," you can rest assured that the words are well marked.

While K'lark is famous for her wise counsel, Chamberlain Olg (nonbinary scuttle bacoo, master of ceremonies) is Hinter's most recognizable voice. An unparalleled organizer, Chamberlain Olg makes sure that each festival runs as much as possible according to plan—and is nimble enough to improvise when things go askew. Chamberlain Olg has a tentacle in every pie, acting as master of ceremonies, contest referee, judge, and constable. The Chamberlain's most prized possession is their magic seashell necklace, which lets their voice echo throughout the town, keeping everyone updated on current events, contest winners, and children gone astray.

Grand Garley (male orpok eater) is known for his physical rather than mental gifts (although he is a cunning strategist and gamesman in his own right). The resident eating champion, this prodigious orpok is the captain of Hinter's storied competitive eating team. Weighing in at a quarter ton, he's widely considered to be the island's greatest athlete. While all comers are welcome to compete at the contest tables under the Feeding Tree, only a few advance past Garley's teammates and get a chance to take on "the Big Bowl" himself.

Hinter's most mysterious celebrity is known simply as the Thief of Thieves. Unfortunately, pickpockets and thieves are common in an environment like Hinter's fairground. However, it's the thieves themselves who have to watch themselves, not the unsuspecting populace. The Thief of Thieves only steals from pickpockets and returns stolen goods. Many a tourist has discovered their purse has been emptied—only to find, later in the day, that every coin has been returned, along with the Thief of Thieves' signature, a petal from a sunset rose.

The more cynical suggest that the Thief of Thieves both perpetrates and repairs the same crimes to gain notoriety. I prefer to believe that the Thief is nothing more than a civic-minded guardian of the town. A very sneaky guardian!

Exploring Hinter

The following is a sample of some of the prominent locations found in Hinter.

The Feeding Tree

If you want to challenge an opponent on the field of battle, look no further than the Feeding Tree. Underneath this majestic tree, you'll find eating contest tables spread with an array of delicacies. The events run rain or shine, so you'll always find a contestant to test your mettle. Besides Grand Garley himself, you'll usually find K'holga the Mound, Mama Keheken, and Spicy Dan plying the boards. If you win a victory against one of Hinter's hungriest, you'll earn (along with a case of indigestion) triple your entrance fee. If you defeat Grand Garley, you'll make a fortune (although this fortune hasn't been claimed in quite a few years!).

Melonchuck Hill

Named for the signature "melon chuck" at the end of Melonfest, Melonchuck Hill is now an integral part of nearly every festival. During Pie Week, chuck a pie for distance! During the Cow Fair, chuck cheese (or a cow if you can)! Or chuck something on the rare non- festival day for fun! Just be careful to watch the skies when you're downslope. Many of Hither's contestants can toss something farther than you'd think!

The Gauntlet

This somewhat grandiose name describes one of Hinter's most inexplicably popular entertainments: the thyalodon rodeo. A recent innovation, it's now a mainstay of nearly every festival. Staying atop a wild thyalodon is difficult enough under the best circumstances, but in the Gauntlet, all the contestants (including the thyalodons) are greased.

As you can imagine, this twist adds to the hilarity of the event for most of the spectators. The cash prize for staying on a thyalodon's back for the full minute is considerable, although not enough to tempt me to try it. It takes ages to get grease out of orpok bristles!

Fiddler's Green

Drink lines at this open- air tavern are usually long, but you can't beat the view from the lawn of the Green, where tables and benches overlook Hinter's main fairground. It's a cool, breezy spot to take in the action at the Feeding Tree, the Gauntlet, and the Melon Chuck, along with the many cooking contests, livestock shows, and taste tests on the main concourse. Not surprisingly, it's a betting hotspot. Here, fortunes have been made and lost on the toss of a gourd, the slip of a greased steed, or the rapid downing of a bloodmouse pie. Be careful who you choose as your betting partner! If you're getting good odds on a seemingly sure thing, you might be throwing away your money on a fixed contest. When in doubt, ask Redbonnet, the galtzagorri proprietor of Fiddler's Green. She'll steer you away from the bad sort.

The Drunk Tank

If you cause too much of a ruckus in Hinter, you'll be visited by Chamberlain Olg's "safety concierges," Hither's hard-as-nails guards and bouncers. At first, you'll simply be given a stern lecture and allowed on your way. However, visitors that continue to cause trouble are deposited in the Drunk Tank, a shallow pool at the bottom of an old quarry. After spending a few hours wading around in chilly water at the bottom of a pit, most of the Drunk Tank's guests have cooled off enough that they don't cause any more trouble, and they're allowed to return to the fair.

Mistvale: Mystery in the Mist.name: 雾谷：迷雾中的谜团 Mistvale: Mystery in the Mist
Mistvale: Mystery in the Mist.text:
Mistvale

Settlement 9

  • CN
  • Town

Fishing and smuggling town

with mysterious disappearances.

Government None

Population 1,023 (34% chochori, 32% xotlxotl, 16% hardriggan, 8% bacoo, 10% others)

Languages Common, Chochori, Aklo, Jotun, Sylvan

Religions Various minor sects, the Balance

Threats creatures in the mist, criminals, malevolent sects, sudden disappearances

Smuggler's Sales If you know the right place to check, you can find illegal goods in Mistvale, occasionally even at a discount. By Gathering Information, it's possible to find illegal items for sale, of up to 12th level. These items cost 70% + 2d8×5% of their usual price. Once an item is found at a given price, it rarely varies without cause.

Captain Itheras farose chochori captain of a crew of Shallows divers

Cuarc female xotlxotl wizard and feeder of the forest

Friend Lello male bannog religious leader of the Third Timers sect

I confess I don't make it out to Mistvale as often as I should! If the good people of Mistvale need a fashionable Seaview hat or a Nilbog cooking pot, blame me. Mistvale is difficult to get to and (forgive me, potential ‘Vale customers) rarely worth the trip.

Mistvale isn't particularly misty—although the same can't be said for the road to the town, through the forest that locals simply call "the Mist." In the perennial fog that blankets the woods, travelers who stray from the road will surely become lost. Mistvale Rift, a canyon that bisects the forest, has an especially evil reputation. On the rare occasions I've crossed the woods, I've kept two eyes out for trouble as I crossed the stone bridge that spans that river of fog!

On most days, the weather in the town of Mistvale itself is perfectly clear, as are its approaches by sea. However, its residents' dispositions are not as sunny, nor is its history.

Two hundred years ago, when Poppy von Barnacle and her lieutenants were establishing settlements all over the Indigo Isles, the Mistvale peninsula was deserted. Sailors from nearby communities sometimes fished offshore but never set foot on the peninsula itself, believing the land was haunted. Perhaps this local superstition should have been a warning to Jesea Swiftarrow, Poppy's intrepid ranger companion—and maybe it was, but for Jesea Swiftarrow, a warning was never anything more than a challenge. And Jesea never shied away from a challenge.

Jesea assembled an expedition drawn from Poppy's pirate fleet and from Bluebell residents looking for a new home. The settlers established a road through the Mist and built the bridge that crosses the Rift. When they emerged from the gloomy woods to the sunny shores of Mistvale Bay, it must have looked like a paradise. And indeed, for the first few years after it was founded, Mistvale flourished. Then, as now, fish were abundant, and divers' baskets overflowed with pearls.

Then, one morning, ten years after the founding of Mistvale, its people vanished.

When a trading ship put into Mistvale Harbor, its crew found a deserted town. Embers still burned in grates; tables were set for breakfast; books lay open on desks… but the townspeople, including Jesea Swiftarrow, were gone.

Search parties from as far away as Rumplank scoured the peninsula but never found any trace of the missing people.

The folk of Bluebell are nothing if not persistent, sometimes to a fault. Three years after the disappearance, a new, smaller wave of settlers moved into Mistvale's empty houses. And again, Mistvale flourished for a few years until its population disappeared once again, just as before. This time, the search for the vanished townspeople was decidedly less thorough—"they baited their hooks for trouble" was the general feeling about the second wave of settlers—and just as inconclusive.

After the second disappearance, Mistvale lay empty for over a hundred years. Eventually, though, it began to collect a new population. One by one, loners, risk-takers, treasure hunters, and misfits moved into the abandoned town. In search of profits, fisherfolk, and pearl hunters braved its evil reputation. Today, Mistvale thrives once more. Nevertheless, the possibility of a third disappearance always hangs over the town.

What caused Mistvale's historical depopulations? Most people speculate that a monster, or perhaps a magical compulsion of some sort, spirited the Mistvale residents into the foggy woods. To this day, treasure seekers venture into the Mist, searching for clues to the mystery. It's said that whoever finds Jesea Swiftarrow's resting place will find a treasury of magic weapons, including her enchanted bow, Deadeye. So far, the search has been fruitless and has cost more than a few lives.

Life in Mistvale

Mistvale is a town with a dark past and an uncertain future, and its nature is reflected in that of its residents. I've found the sailors and fisher folk of the town (primarily chochori and xotlxotl) to be dour and unfriendly to outsiders. When I visit, I usually find a market for Nilbog ironworks such as fish hooks, shovels, and locks (never was there such a town with so many locks on doors), but not much appetite for fripperies such as fashionable clothes and toys. Mistvale is not much of a family town. Perhaps few want to raise children under the shadow of its cursed past.

The fishing off of Mistvale Point is excellent, which is one of the reasons why the town has been repeatedly repopulated against all good sense. All Mistvale fishers are good swimmers, and most are amphibious xotlxotl or chochori, but I also have had the good fortune to witness the Mistvale fishing technique, which is unique in my experience. When a boat finds a school of fish, its crew dives overboard, carrying a massive net between them. They race through the water, scooping up any marine life in their path. Rarely does a Mistvale fishing boat return home empty-handed.

Treasure diving is another local industry. The Shallows, an area of coral reefs off of the Beacon Cliffs, is the source for much of the pearl and coral jewelry that adorns the people of the Indigo Isles. The reefs also yield other valuables; many a ship has foundered in the Shallows, and divers plunder coins and other treasures from their smashed hulls.

That pretty much covers the legal industries in Mistvale. The extent of illegal activity is not for me to say, but on moonlit nights, lights sometimes flash from strange ships—ships that never enter the harbor but seem to know their way through the mazelike reefs of the Shallows. I have nothing more to say about them except that an honest trader can't compete in a town awash in smuggled goods.

Mistvale's closest neighbor and trading partner is Hinter. Each day, barges carry the day's catch—fish, coral, pearls, and who knows what other cargo—east to Hinter Market, where it is packed off toward Seaview.

When Mistvalians return home after a day's labors, they indulge in many charming pursuits: drinking in the Third Time Tavern or the Shallow Draught, glaring at strangers, and indulging in a taste for... alternative religions. There seem to be as many foreboding sects in Mistvale as there are citizens. The Children of the Mist, the Gray Order, and those wild Third Timers are the most public of these societies, but I've heard rumors of several others.

People of Mistvale

Mistvale's citizens are independent types, and they have no centralized government that I've been able to discover. That said, as elsewhere on the Indigo Isles, ship captains tend to carry their authority with them even when ashore. When trouble rears its head, the first person to be notified is usually Captain Itheras (farose chochori captain), the leader of a crew of Shallows divers. They're no diplomat, but at least they keep the peace. When some local toughs were playing keep-away with my pack, the captain sent them packing and returned my property intact. They were so polite that I must refrain from spelling out any suspicions I may have about their crew's activities in the Shallows. Cuarc (female xotlxotl wizard) is perhaps the nicest person in all of Mistvale, which isn't saying a lot. She has been nothing but friendly to me, and as a wizard, she always needs the kind of odds and ends that I can supply. That said, she has her share of eccentricities. Each morning she brings a plump bag into the Mist and returns empty-handed. She claims that she does it to "keep the forest fed," although she flatly refuses to reveal what's in the bag. Whatever it is, I'm quite against letting the forest go hungry, especially while I'm visiting!

When I said that Cuarc was the nicest person in town, Friend Lello (male bannog religious leader) must have slipped my mind. As his name would suggest, he's quite friendly—almost overwhelmingly so. He and his disciples are members of a sect called the Third Timers, which believes that the people who disappeared from Mistvale live in a paradise deep in the foggy woods. The Third Timers like to parade into the forest to dance and revel—and against all odds, they always seem to come out again. Unlike most of the community's residents, Friend Lello and his sect look forward to a third mass disappearance and seek to hasten it through their songs, dances, and prayers.

Exploring Mistvale

The following is a sample of some of the prominent locations found in Mistvale.

The Mist

The Mist sprawls over a ring of hills surrounding a bowl-shaped valley. This miles-wide forested depression brims with fog except during blinding rainstorms. In the middle of the forest, Mistvale Rift cuts the Mistvale peninsula off from the rest of the island. The Hinter Road, with its stone bridge across the rift, is considered the only safe passage through the Mist. No one travels on the Seaview Road anymore—who knows if its old rope bridge still spans the Rift?

The Rift is a wondrous and frightening sight. Imagine a canyon three hundred feet wide with cliffs that drop straight down and quickly disappear into the rolling mist below. Although the Rift stretches for miles, no bottom can be seen through the blanket of mist. Any speech quieter than a shout is drowned out by the hiss of the waterfalls that pour out into the canyon, and the humidity is so thick that walking is a constant struggle. However, the bridge spanning the Rift seems incredibly strong and well-maintained. I must confess that I have no idea how it was built in the first place, but I try not to linger on the bridge any longer than necessary and ponder such thoughts.

While all experienced Mistvale travelers are too wise to leave the main road, disappearances are common. Monsters lurk in the woods; that's certain. In my own travels, I have seen shadows in the mist—large humanoid figures that stood still and seemed to watch us as we passed. There are rumors of bigger and more frightening creatures in the forest as well—rumors that I believe wholeheartedly.

The Shallows

Not far from Mistvale Harbor are the Shallows, an area of coral reefs facing a sheer cliffside. Before Mistvale's lighthouse was erected, many ships went aground on those deadly reefs or smashed into its inhospitable cliffs. For all that, I understand that the Shallows are a wonderful place to swim, offering vistas of pink and purple coral, pearl oysters for the taking, and even a picturesque wreck or two to explore. Of course, I know none of this from experience. While no laws restrict entrance to the Shallows, locals frown upon diving by interlopers, and outsiders who trespass often meet with unfortunate "accidents."

If rumors are true, there might be reasons for keeping strangers away from the Shallows beyond a desire to monopolize the pearl supply. The Shallows' maze of hull-ripping reefs is impassable except by local sailors, making it a perfect base for smuggling operations. Perhaps not all of the treasures of the Shallows are dredged up from sunken ships; some might be purchased from the holds of Goldcrop pirates instead.

Whenever a Mistvalian walks into town with an object of uncertain provenance, they claim it's "bounty from the Shallows." I dare say that's true, in one sense or another. Whether buried or stolen, the Shallows provide many riches.

The Shallow Draught

The Shallow Draught must be the quietest tavern on Bluebell—at least when a stranger like me walks through the doors. Grizzled sailors nurse their drink in silence; ordering a round from Karngoss (carbor chochori tavernkeeper), the proprietor, is a matter of eye contact and subtle hand gestures.

For all its gloom, the Shallow Draught is packed on most nights (there's no accounting for tastes). Its location, on a clifftop above the Shallows, makes it a popular choice for treasure divers; that rickety wooden stairway down to the reefs sees a lot of traffic, though I wouldn't trust my life to it.
